problem_statement.Title: ccode(sinc(x)) emitted as unsupported comment + literal 'sinc(x)' instead of portable sin(x)/x piecewise expression
problem_statement.Description: When printing a SymPy sinc(x) using ccode, the C printer marks sinc as unsupported and emits a comment plus the literal function call (e.g. "// Not supported in C:\n// sinc\nsinc(x)") instead of a portable C expression that equals sin(x)/x with the correct value at x == 0.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. CCodePrinter does not provide a handler for sinc: sympy/printing/ccode.py (class CCodePrinter) has no _print_sinc and the 'sinc' name is not present in CCodePrinter.known_functions. This causes dispatch to fall through.

2. Generic fallback treats unknown Function objects as unsupported: sympy/printing/codeprinter.py:CodePrinter._print_Function checks known_functions and, if absent, calls _print_not_supported which records the unsupported function and returns the emptyPrinter output. This produces the observed comment and raw 'sinc(x)'.

3. SymPy's sinc semantics are sin(arg)/arg with a special-case at arg == 0: sympy/functions/elementary/trigonometric.py (class sinc) implements _eval_rewrite_as_sin (rewrites to sin(arg)/arg) and eval (returns 1 when arg == 0). Printers should preserve that semantics rather than emitting a literal 'sinc' call.

4. The correct C-level representation is a Piecewise expressing sin(arg)/arg for arg != 0 and 1 for arg == 0. Concretely: Piecewise((sin(arg)/arg, Ne(arg, 0)), (1, True)). When formatted by the existing Piecewise printer this produces the exact C-style ternary form, for example:
"((Ne(theta, 0)) ? (\n   sin(theta)/theta\n)\n: (\n   1\n))".

5. The missing handler is the single language-specific omission: other printers (e.g. OctaveCodePrinter._print_sinc in sympy/printing/octave.py) implement language-specific _print_sinc methods, demonstrating the established pattern for addressing such cases. The C printer deviates by lacking its own _print_sinc.
StepsToReproduce:
In a Python session with SymPy installed, run:
from sympy import symbols, sinc
from sympy.printing.ccode import ccode
x = symbols('x')
print(ccode(sinc(x)))

Optional: verify Piecewise printing works by running:
from sympy import Piecewise, sin, Ne
print(ccode(Piecewise((sin(x)/x, Ne(x, 0)), (1, True))))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
ccode(sinc(x)) should emit a portable C expression equivalent to sin(x)/x for x != 0 and 1 for x == 0. In user-visible terms it should print the Piecewise/ternary form (the CCodePrinter's Piecewise formatting), for example:
((Ne(x, 0)) ? (
   sin(x)/x
)
: (
   1
))

problem_statement.Title: trigsimp does not rewrite pure-exponential forms into trig (missing exp->trig pass)
problem_statement.Description: trigsimp leaves purely-exponential representations of trigonometric identities unchanged. Example: (-I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k))/2 is not converted to sin(k), and the divided-by-k form is not converted to the corresponding trig form.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing invocation of exptrigsimp in sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py::trigsimp: trigsimp calls expr._eval_trigsimp(**opts) but does not run the module's exponential→trig rewrite pass, so pure-exponential inputs never get the exp->trig conversion. (Effect: (-I*exp(I*k)+I*exp(-I*k))/2 remains exponential instead of becoming sin(k).)

2) Wrong gating conditional inside sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py::exptrigsimp (exp_trig helper): code appends e.rewrite(exp) only when e.has(*_trigs). This encodes the incorrect assumption "only attempt exp→trig rewrites when trig/hyperbolic atoms are already present" and causes pure-exponential expressions to be skipped.

3) Lower-level matching/groebner branches in sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py (e.g., __trigsimp, trigsimp_groebner, futrig, trigsimp_old) also gate exp-aware rewrites on expr.has(*_trigs). These guards repeat the same incorrect assumption and prevent the rewrite paths for pure-exponential inputs.

4) exptrigsimp already contains the correct conversion logic and a safety guard: it will convert exponentials to trig forms (exptrigsimp's rewrite would map (-I*exp(I*k)+I*exp(-I*k))/2 -> sin(k) and similarly produce sin(k)/k for the divided form). It also enforces the safety check if newexpr.has(I) and not expr.has(I): return expr, i.e., it refuses conversions that would introduce I into an originally real expression. This shows the conversion logic exists and is guarded for safety; the problem is that trigsimp never reaches that logic for pure-exponential inputs.

5) Post-conversion behavior: trigsimp currently does not convert sin(x)/x -> sinc(x) by default. After exp->trig conversion, the expression (-I*exp(I*k)+I*exp(-I*k))/(2*k) would simplify to sin(k)/k, but not to sinc(k); sinc conversion is a separate, opt-in/optional rewrite and is not part of the failing assumption above.
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with SymPy available.
from sympy import symbols, exp, I, trigsimp
trigsimp(f)  # expected sin(k), observed remains in exponential form
trigsimp(f2)  # expected sin(k)/k (or optionally sinc(k)), observed remains exponential divided by k
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: trigsimp should convert exponential representations of trig functions into trig form. For the examples above, trigsimp((-I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k))/2) should return sin(k). trigsimp(( -I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k) )/(2*k)) should return sin(k)/k (sinc(k) is an optional/opt-in rewrite and not required by default).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: trigsimp leaves the expressions in exponential form. trigsimp((-I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k))/2) returns (-I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k))/2. trigsimp(( -I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k) )/(2*k)) returns (-I*exp(I*k) + I*exp(-I*k))/(2*k) (i.e., no exp->trig conversion is performed).

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX printer prints negative function exponents as reciprocals and expands denominators, causing mismatch with pretty printer
problem_statement.Description: The LaTeX printer sometimes produces output that is inconsistent with the pretty printer: negative function exponents (e.g. exp(-x)) are rendered as reciprocals (1/e^{x}) and multiplicative grouping is expanded (e.g. 1/(x+y)/2 -> 1/(2 x + 2 y)), resulting in different visual/output forms for the same expression.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/latex.py:LatexPrinter._print_Mul: Assumes using sympy.simplify.radsimp.fraction() (and then LaTeX-specific fraction heuristics) is the correct policy to decide numerator vs denominator factors for printing. This allows function-based negative exponents (e.g. exp(-x)) to be treated as denominator factors and printed as a reciprocal (\frac{1}{e^{x}}), which differs from the pretty printer's policy.

2) sympy/simplify/radsimp.py:fraction: The fraction() routine can split expressions like exp(-x) into (1, exp(x)) in its non-exact/default mode (fraction(exp(-x)) -> (1, exp(x))), while fraction(exp(-x), exact=True) -> (exp(-x), 1). Relying on fraction()'s decomposition (without the consistent policy of the pretty printer) lets function negative exponents be moved to the denominator.

3) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py:PrettyPrinter._print_Mul: Encodes the intended policy for printing: only commutative Pow objects whose exponent is a rational negative number (item.is_Pow and item.exp.is_Rational and item.exp.is_negative) are moved to the denominator, and Rational factors are split by as_numer_denom(); general function calls with negative exponents (e.g. exp(-x)) remain in the numerator and are printed as e^{-x}. LatexPrinter does not use that same factor-collection policy, causing the discrepancy.

4) sympy/printing/latex.py:LatexPrinter._print_Mul (formatting heuristics): After fraction()-based decomposition, LatexPrinter applies long_frac_ratio and fold_short_frac heuristics which can prefer \frac{...}{...} formatting; combined with the fraction() decomposition this produces outputs like '\\frac{1}{e^{x}} \\log(...)' and '\\frac{1}{2 x + 2 y}'. The presence and values of these heuristics (long_frac_ratio == 2, fold_short_frac True/False) affect whether the reciprocal/fraction form is chosen but are not the root cause of factor movement.

5) Mismatch of factor-collection rules (location: latex.py:_print_Mul vs pretty.py:_print_Mul) is the concrete cause of the user-observed examples: latex(exp(-x)*log(x)) yields '\\frac{1}{e^{x}} \\log{\\left (x \\right )}' while pretty prints e^{-x}⋅log(x); similarly, latex(1/(x+y)/2) yields '\\frac{1}{2 x + 2 y}' while pretty prints a fraction with denominator 2⋅(x + y).
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import var, exp, log, latex, pprint
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
The LaTeX printer should produce the same visible output as the pretty printer for these expressions (unless LaTeX can produce a clearly better representation). Concretely:
- latex(exp(-x)*log(x)) should render as e^{-x}⋅log(x) (i.e., not as a reciprocal \frac{1}{e^{x}}\log(x)).
- latex(1/(x + y)/2) should present the same grouping as the pretty printer (a single denominator 2*(x + y) rather than expanded 2 x + 2 y).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: latex(exp(-x)*log(x)) returns '\\frac{1}{e^{x}} \\log{\\left (x \\right )}' while pprint prints e^{-x}⋅log(x). latex(1/(x + y)/2) returns '\\frac{1}{2 x + 2 y}' while pprint prints a fraction with denominator 2⋅(x + y).

hints_text:

problem_statement.Title: Mathematica printer emits non-Mathematica Derivative and Float formats
problem_statement.Description: sympy's Mathematica code printer (MCodePrinter) prints Derivative and Float values using StrPrinter defaults rather than Mathematica syntax: derivatives remain "Derivative(f(t), t)" instead of Mathematica's D[f[t], t], and floats in scientific notation remain "1.0e-4" instead of Mathematica's "1.0*^-4".
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) MCodePrinter does not override printing for Derivative. Location: sympy/printing/mathematica.py::MCodePrinter. Incorrect assumption: inherited StrPrinter representation "Derivative(%s)" is acceptable for Mathematica output. The correct Mathematica representation is D[...]; equivalently the printing relationship should be D[%s] where the arguments are comma-separated (e.g. D[f[t], t]).

2) MCodePrinter does not override printing for Float. Location: sympy/printing/mathematica.py::MCodePrinter. Incorrect assumption: inherited StrPrinter float formatting (which uses an 'e' exponent marker) is acceptable for Mathematica. The correct relationship for scientific notation in Mathematica uses '*^' as the exponent marker: convert a printed mantissa/exponent of the form "<mantissa>e<exponent>" to "<mantissa>*^<exponent>" (with the exponent canonicalized, e.g. '-04' -> '-4').

3) The StrPrinter implementations encode the wrong conventions for the Mathematica target. Locations: sympy/printing/str.py::StrPrinter._print_Derivative (produces "Derivative(...)") and sympy/printing/str.py::StrPrinter._print_Float (produces 'e'-notation). Evidence: these implementations produce the observed outputs. Relevant pattern: sympy/printing/fcode.py::FCodePrinter._print_Float shows the canonical approach — call CodePrinter._print_Float(self, expr) then translate the exponent marker (for Fortran 'e'->'d'); the Mathematica printer must follow the same minimal-change relationship ('e'->'*^') and optionally canonicalize the exponent via int().
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with sympy available.
Define a symbolic function and symbol, e.g. t = Symbol('t'); f = Function('f').
Construct expressions: expr1 = Derivative(f(t), t); expr2 = Float('1.0e-4') (or Float(1.0e-4)).
Use the Mathematica code printer to convert them to strings (e.g. via MCodePrinter().doprint(expr) or the repository's mathematica-code helper).
Observe the outputs for expr1 and expr2.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The Mathematica code printer should emit valid Mathematica syntax: a derivative prints as D[f[t], t] (using D[...]) and floats in scientific notation use Mathematica's exponent marker, e.g. 1.0*^-4.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Derivatives are printed using the generic StrPrinter form, e.g. "Derivative(f(t), t)". Floats in scientific notation are printed with an 'e' exponent, e.g. "1.0e-4".

problem_statement.Title: apart() drops symbolic scalar multiplier due to incorrect gens handling in composite domain construction
problem_statement.Description: Calling Expr.apart(t) on expressions with a symbolic scalar multiplier (e.g. a * (...) / (2*t - 1)) can produce an incorrect result where the scalar 'a' is lost or misplaced. The incorrect result appears when apart dispatches into the polynomial/Poly construction pipeline.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect use of gens returned by parallel_dict_from_basic in sympy/polys/constructor.py::_construct_composite: the code does "polys, gens = parallel_dict_from_basic(numers + denoms)" and then uses those gens unchanged. This assumes the discovered coefficient gens are disjoint from the polynomial main generators (opt.gens). That assumption is false for the reproducer and allows coefficient gens to overlap polynomial gens, corrupting the representation.

2) Reliance on the invariant len(gens) == monom tuple length without preserving it: _construct_composite computes n = len(gens) and zeros = (0,)*n and indexes monom tuples (e.g. denom[zeros]) assuming monom tuple length equals len(gens). When gens overlap or must be reduced, the monom tuple layout no longer matches these assumptions, causing coefficients (scalar multipliers) to be lost or misplaced.

3) Missing reordering of existing polys when gens change in _construct_composite (sympy/polys/constructor.py::_construct_composite): the code does not call the existing helper to reorder monom/coeff dicts to match a filtered or re-ordered gens, so monom→coefficient mapping becomes inconsistent.

4) Caller-side assumption about parallel_dict_from_basic (sympy/polys/polyutils.py:parallel_dict_from_basic): callers (e.g. _construct_composite) assume parallel_dict_from_basic will not return gens that conflict with the outer polynomial gens unless the caller constrains it. In practice parallel_dict_from_basic can choose gens overlapping opt.gens unless prevented.

5) Upstream reliance on construct_domain/Poly._from_dict invariant (sympy/polys/polytools.py:Poly._from_dict): Poly._from_dict assumes construct_domain produces a domain and rep whose monom tuples match the gens ordering it will use; if construct_domain/_construct_composite returns inconsistent gens/rep mapping, downstream Poly construction and arithmetic (cancel/div/apart) produce incorrect user-visible results.

6) Correct relationship that must hold (diagnostic): the coefficient gens used to build the composite domain must exclude the polynomial's main gens (opt.gens), and whenever gens are changed or filtered the existing polys dicts must be reordered so that len(gens) == monom tuple length and monom tuple indexing remains consistent. The code currently uses gens as returned (wrong) instead of the required filtered/reordered gens (correct).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: apart(t) should preserve the scalar multiplier 'a' and return a * (the same partial-fraction decomposition obtained when a is substituted to 1). For the reproducer, expected: a * (-t + 1/2 - 1/(2*(2*t - 1))).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: bug.subs(a,1).apart() returns -t + 1/2 - 1/(2*(2*t - 1)), but bug.apart(t) returns -a*t (the scalar 'a' is lost/misplaced), so the result does not equal a * bug.subs(a,1).apart().

I've already been working on the issue for several days and going to make a pull request soon. The problem is that a domain of a fraction is identified as a ZZ[y] in this example:
`In [9]: apart((x+y)/(2*x-y), x)`
`Out[9]: 0`

If I manually change the automatically detected domain to a QQ[y], it works correctly, but I fail on several tests, e.g. `assert ZZ[x].get_field() == ZZ.frac_field(x)`. 
The problem is that a ZZ[y] Ring is converted to a ZZ(y) Field. The division using DMP algorithm is done correctly, however during following conversion to a basic polynomial non-integers (1/2, 3/2 in this case) are considered to be a 0.

I have also compared to different expressions: (x+1)/(2*x-4), (x+y)/(2*x-y) and apart them on x. The differences appear while converting a Ring to a Field: `get_field(self)` method is different for each class.
It simply returns QQ for the IntegerRing, which is mathematically correct; while for PolynomialRing the code is more complicated. It initializes a new PolyRing class, which preprocesses domain according to it’s options and returns a new class: Rational function field in y over ZZ with lex order. So the polynomial with a fractional result is never calculated.

I guess the ZZ[y] Ring should be converted to a QQ(y) Field, since division is only defined for the Fields, not Rings.
@ankibues Well, your solution simply converts a decomposition on one variable (t in this case) to a decomposition on two variables (a, t), which leads to `NotImplementedError: multivariate partial fraction decomposition` for the `bug.apart(t)` code. 
Moreover, the problem is not only in the apart() function, e.g.
`In [20]: Poly(x + y, x).div(Poly(2*x - y, x))`
`Out[20]: (Poly(0, x, domain='ZZ[y]'), Poly(0, x, domain='ZZ[y]'))`
In this case the division is also done incorrectly. The domain is still ZZ[y] here, while it should be QQ[y] for getting an answer.
By the way, this `In [21]: apart((x+y)/(2.0*x-y),x)` works well:
`Out[21]: 1.5*y/(2.0*x - 1.0*y + 0.5`
  
And if I change the default Field for each Ring to QQ, the result is correct:
`Out[22]: 3*y/(2*(2*x - y)) + 1/2`

problem_statement.Title: Nested symbolic sums over Identity matrix entries return 0 instead of n
problem_statement.Description: When M is assumed orthogonal and e = refine((M.T * M).doit()) yields an Identity matrix, a nested symbolic double-sum of e[i,j] over i and j evaluates to 0 instead of the expected n.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py :: Identity._entry - The method encodes index equality by Python-level object equality (if i == j: return S.One else: return S.Zero). This assumes that different Symbol objects are always unequal, so for distinct symbolic dummy indices Identity._entry returns the literal S.Zero instead of a symbolic Kronecker delta.

2) The literal S.Zero returned by Identity._entry for non-identical index objects encodes the incorrect assumption "different Symbol objects mean different indices". That hardcoded zero removes the Kronecker-delta structure needed for symbolic summation and causes nested Sum reductions to miss delta simplification.

3) sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py :: MatrixElement.doit and MatrixExpr.__getitem__ - These methods delegate to the parent's _entry implementation, so the wrong scalar (S.Zero) produced by Identity._entry is what Sum receives and attempts to reduce.

4) sympy/concrete/summations.py and sympy/concrete/delta.py - The summation engine already has explicit support for reducing KroneckerDelta expressions. Therefore the correct relationship for Identity entries when indices are symbolic is to expose a KroneckerDelta(i, j) so the summation routines can perform the expected simplification. Concretely, Identity._entry should return S.One when i and j are the same Python object (or concrete equal indices) and return KroneckerDelta(i, j) otherwise (instead of returning S.Zero).
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import Q as Query
print(Sum(Sum(e[i, j], (i, 0, n-1)), (j, 0, n-1)).doit())  # currently prints 0
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Given M assumed orthogonal and e = refine((M.T * M).doit()) evaluates to an identity matrix of size n, the total sum of all elements of e (double sum over i and j from 0 to n-1) should evaluate to n.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Under the current implementation, the nested symbolic sum Sum(Sum(e[i, j], (i, 0, n-1)), (j, 0, n-1)).doit() evaluates to 0 instead of n because Identity._entry returns S.Zero for distinct symbolic index objects.

That's because you are using literal values for `i` and `j`: `Eq(i,j)` when `i=1`, `j=2` gives `S.false`.

The modifications that I suggested work only if you don't nest the Sums; I'm not sure why the Sums don't denest when Piecewise is involved but (as @wakita demonstrated) it *is* smart enough when using KroneckerDelta to denest, but it doesn't evaluate until a concrete value is substituted.

```
>>> from sympy import Q as Query

3

>>> _.subs(n,3)

>>> _.doit()
Piecewise((3, Eq(i, 0)), (0, True)) + Piecewise((3, Eq(i, 1)), (0, True)) + Piecewise((3, Eq(i, 2)), (0, True))

# the KroneckerDelta form denests but doesn't evaluate until you substitute a concrete value

>>> Sum(Sum(KroneckerDelta(i,j), (i, 0, n-1)), (j, 0, n-1))
Sum(KroneckerDelta(i, j), (i, 0, n - 1), (j, 0, n - 1))
>>> _.doit()
Sum(Piecewise((1, (0 <= j) & (j <= n - 1)), (0, True)), (j, 0, n - 1))
>>> _.subs(n,3)
Sum(Piecewise((1, (0 <= j) & (j <= 2)), (0, True)), (j, 0, 2))
>>> _.doit()
3
```

Because of the better behavior of KroneckerDelta, perhaps the `_entry` should be written in terms of that instead of Piecewise.

problem_statement.Title: Matrix.is_upper raises IndexError for tall matrices (rows > cols)
problem_statement.Description: Matrix.is_upper (and the related _eval_is_upper_hessenberg) can raise IndexError when run on tall matrices (more rows than columns). The property checks iterate column indices up to i (or i-1) without bounding by the actual number of columns, causing out-of-range indexing.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/matrices/matrices.py:MatrixProperties.is_upper — Incorrect assumption: the inner loop uses for j in range(i) which assumes self.cols >= i. On tall matrices (e.g., 4x2) this produces j values >= self.cols (e.g., (3,2)), leading to out-of-range indexing. The correct relationship for the column loop bound is range(min(i, self.cols)).

2) sympy/matrices/matrices.py:MatrixProperties._eval_is_upper_hessenberg — Incorrect assumption: the inner loop uses for j in range(i - 1) which assumes self.cols > (i - 2). This likewise can produce j values >= self.cols on tall matrices. The correct relationship for the column loop bound is range(min(i - 1, self.cols)).

3) sympy/matrices/matrices.py:MatrixShaping.extract and sympy/matrices/dense.py:DenseMatrix.__getitem__ — These call a2idx to validate column indices. extract converts requested column indices via colsList = [a2idx(k, self.cols) for k in colsList]; when callers supply j >= self.cols, a2idx raises IndexError. This is the site where the invalid index is surfaced.

4) sympy/matrices/matrices.py:a2idx — Enforces index validity (if n is not None, requires 0 <= j < n) and raises IndexError("Index out of range: a[%s]" % (j,)) when that contract is violated. a2idx is behaving as intended; the defect is that callers generate indices outside its valid range.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL with SymPy available.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling is_upper on a 4x2 zero matrix (and on other tall matrices) should return a boolean (True for the 4x2 zero matrix) indicating whether all entries below the main diagonal are zero, and should not raise an IndexError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling is_upper on a 4x2 zero matrix raises IndexError: "Index out of range: a[2]". The traceback shows the failure occurs when the property code attempts to access an element with a column index >= number of columns (via DenseMatrix.__getitem__ -> extract -> a2idx).

problem_statement.Title: Permutation constructor raises ValueError for non-disjoint cycles (e.g. Permutation([[0,1],[0,1]]))
problem_statement.Description: Calling Permutation with a list of cycles that share elements (non-disjoint cycles) raises a ValueError instead of composing the cycles and returning the resulting permutation (for example, Permutation([[0,1],[0,1]]) raises but should return the identity permutation). The failure occurs in the Permutation constructor when cyclic-form input is passed.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect rejection of cross-cycle duplicates in Permutation.__new__ (sympy/combinatorics/permutations.py : Permutation.__new__). The constructor flattens the cycle arguments (temp = flatten(args)) and does if has_dups(temp): if is_cycle: raise ValueError('there were repeated elements; to resolve cycles use Cycle%s.' ...). This encodes the false assumption that cycles must be disjoint across separate cycle tuples. The correct relationship (as used elsewhere in the code) is that duplicates across different cycles are allowed when input is in cyclic form because cycles will be composed, so only array-form inputs (not is_cycle) should be rejected for duplicate elements.

2) Cycle composition already implements the intended semantics (sympy/combinatorics/permutations.py : Cycle.__call__, Cycle.list). Cycle objects compose successive cycles and return an array form. Concrete evidence: for args [[0,1],[0,1]] flatten(args) == [0,1,0,1] and composing via Cycle yields aform == [0,1] (the identity). The premature duplicate check prevents this correct composition.

3) Per-cycle uniqueness check is correct and must remain (sympy/combinatorics/permutations.py : Cycle.__init__). Cycle.__init__ rightfully raises ValueError('All elements must be unique in a cycle.') when a single cycle tuple contains duplicate elements; that restriction is distinct from and should not be conflated with cross-cycle duplicates.

4) No incorrect assumptions found in parent/basic methods relying on array form (sympy/core/basic.py : Basic.__getnewargs__, Basic.__hash__; sympy/combinatorics/permutations.py : Permutation._hashable_content). These continue to expect the final array_form produced by composing cycles, so removing the cross-cycle duplicate rejection does not violate their expectations.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL or script with sympy in the import path.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The constructor composes the provided cycles left-to-right and returns the resulting Permutation (for Permutation([[0,1],[0,1]]) the result should be the identity permutation, i.e. array form [0, 1]). Non-disjoint elements across separate cycle tuples are allowed and interpreted by composing the cycles.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Permutation([[0,1],[0,1]]) raises ValueError with the message 'there were repeated elements; to resolve cycles use Cycle%s.' instead of returning the composed permutation.

problem_statement.Title: Matrix.hstack/vstack dispatch uses first-argument class causing wrong shapes for zero-dimension matrices (sympy 1.1)
problem_statement.Description: In sympy 1.1 Matrix.hstack and Matrix.vstack produce incorrect shapes when the first argument is a zero-dimension matrix. Example: hstack of matrices with shapes (0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(0,3) yields (0,3) instead of the expected (0,6). The issue did not occur in sympy 1.0.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect dispatch assumption in MatrixShaping.hstack: sympy/matrices/common.py::MatrixShaping.hstack uses the runtime type of the first argument (kls = type(args[0])) and calls reduce(kls.row_join, args). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the first-argument class should determine the join implementation for the whole reduction. The correct relationship is to use the caller class (cls) for the reduction (i.e., reduce(cls.row_join, args)).

2) Incorrect dispatch assumption in MatrixShaping.vstack: sympy/matrices/common.py::MatrixShaping.vstack uses the runtime type of the first argument (kls = type(args[0])) and calls reduce(kls.col_join, args). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the first-argument class should determine the join implementation for the whole reduction. The correct relationship is to use the caller class (cls) for the reduction (i.e., reduce(cls.col_join, args)).

3) row_join/_eval_row_join and col_join/_eval_col_join compute shapes and select the concrete result class correctly: sympy/matrices/common.py::MatrixShaping.row_join/_eval_row_join and ::col_join/_eval_col_join perform correct shape arithmetic and construct results via classof(self, other)._new(...). Therefore the shape mis-aggregation is caused by the wrong reduce dispatch, not by arithmetic in the join methods.

4) The incorrect dispatch pattern is localized: the only call sites found using kls = type(args[0]) + reduce(...) are MatrixShaping.hstack and MatrixShaping.vstack in sympy/matrices/common.py. These two locations encode the defect.

5) Investigation/testing gap: the presented analysis did not perform a concrete runtime trace of reduce with the user-provided zero-dimension inputs nor run the matrix test suite to validate no regressions. This missing verification means the minimal change has not been proven at runtime in the investigation.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import sympy as sy; M1=sy.Matrix.zeros(0,0); M2=sy.Matrix.zeros(0,1); M3=sy.Matrix.zeros(0,2); M4=sy.Matrix.zeros(0,3); print(sy.Matrix.hstack(M1, M2, M3, M4).shape)"
python -c "import sympy as sy; M1=sy.Matrix.zeros(1,0); M2=sy.Matrix.zeros(1,1); M3=sy.Matrix.zeros(1,2); M4=sy.Matrix.zeros(1,3); print(sy.Matrix.hstack(M1, M2, M3, M4).shape)"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: hstack should return a matrix whose shape reflects the sum of the input columns and (for row counts) the consistent row dimension. Example: Matrix.hstack((0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(0,3)).shape should be (0,6). For one-row inputs, Matrix.hstack((1,0),(1,1),(1,2),(1,3)).shape should be (1,6).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On sympy 1.1, Matrix.hstack(Matrix.zeros(0,0), Matrix.zeros(0,1), Matrix.zeros(0,2), Matrix.zeros(0,3)).shape returns (0,3) instead of (0,6). The similar one-row case returns (1,6) as expected.

```
>>> import sympy as sy

'1.1.1rc1'
```

I am away at a conference. The change should be almost identical to the fix for dense matrices, if someone can manage to get a patch in. I *might* be able to do it tomorrow.
Okay.  I've looked this over and its convoluted...

`SparseMatrix` should impliment `_eval_col_join`.  `col_join` should not be implemented.  It is, and that is what `hstack` is calling, which is why my previous patch didn't fix `SparseMatrix`s as well.  However, the patch that @asmeurer referenced ensures that `SparseMatrix.row_join(DenseMatrix)` returns a `SparseMatrix` whereas `CommonMatrix.row_join(SparseMatrix, DenseMatrix)` returns a `classof(SparseMatrix, DenseMatrix)` which happens to be a `DenseMatrix`.  I don't think that these should behave differently.  This API needs to be better thought out.
So is there a simple fix that can be made for the release or should this be postponed?

problem_statement.Title: decompose(..., separate=True) returns non-deterministic / arbitrary-ordered lists
problem_statement.Description: The decompose() function in sympy/integrals/intpoly.py, when called with separate=True, returns a list of monomials whose order depends on expr.args iteration or dict.values() iteration rather than an explicit deterministic ordering. This yields flaky/ordering-dependent behavior observed in tests and callers.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: expr.args order is a valid, stable ordering for monomials. Location: sympy/integrals/intpoly.py::decompose (Add-case early-return). The code returns [monom[1] for monom in degrees], which preserves expr.args order instead of producing a deterministic ordering. The correct relationship for ordering monomials is based on total degree computed as sum(degree_list(monom, *symbols)) with a deterministic tie-breaker (see item 2).

2) Incorrect assumption: dict.values() iteration yields a canonical ordering. Location: sympy/integrals/intpoly.py::decompose (final fall-through branch). The code returns list(poly_dict.values()) which depends on dict key iteration order. The intended/diagnosed deterministic ordering should be: primary key = total degree (ascending) where degree = sum(degree_list(monom, *symbols)), secondary key = SymPy canonical expression ordering (monom.sort_key()), with a stable fallback (e.g. str(monom)). An explicit form for the final branch would be iterating keys in a deterministic sorted order (e.g. [poly_dict[k] for k in sorted(poly_dict.keys())]) or otherwise returning values sorted by (degree, monom.sort_key()).

3) Tests encode the expectation of deterministic ordering. Location: sympy/integrals/tests/test_intpoly.py (assertions like decompose(x + y, True) == [x, y] and other ordered-list assertions). The tests assume decompose(..., True) is deterministic; the implementation does not guarantee that.

4) Calling sites propagate the nondeterminism. Location: sympy/integrals/intpoly.py::polytope_integrate (calls decompose(polys, separate=True) and assigns monoms = decompose(..., True)). Because polytope_integrate uses decompose(..., True) results, the arbitrary ordering may be observed by higher-level callers/tests.

5) Docstring / implementation mismatch: the decompose docstring implies a specific ordering in examples, but the implementation lacks an explicit ordering policy, leading to inconsistent outputs across different code paths (Add early-return vs final branch). Location: sympy/integrals/intpoly.py::decompose (docstring vs implementation).

6) Investigation/compatibility gap (proposal assumption): proposed deterministic tie-breaker uses monom.sort_key(); availability/behavior of sort_key() across supported SymPy/Python versions was not verified by the investigation. Location: proposed change to sympy/integrals/intpoly.py::decompose (fix proposal). This gap may affect portability/compatibility of any change that depends on sort_key().
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with the SymPy package installed.
Print the result: print(decompose(x + y, True)) — observe that the returned list may be [x, y] or [y, x] depending on iteration order.
Alternatively, run the intpoly test file that asserts ordering (e.g. pytest sympy/integrals/tests/test_intpoly.py) and observe failures that depend on the returned list order.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When called with separate=True, decompose(...) returns a deterministic, well-defined list of monomials in a stable order (consistent across runs and independent of dict/expr.args insertion/iteration order). The ordering should be predictable for callers and tests (for example: sorted by total degree ascending with a deterministic tie-breaker such as SymPy's canonical expression ordering).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: decompose(..., separate=True) returns lists whose order is determined by expr.args iteration in the Add-case or by dict.values() iteration in the final branch. The order is incidental/arbitrary and can change, causing test failures and nondeterministic observable behavior.

problem_statement.Title: Like terms with numerically-equal Float exponents are not combined (e.g. -0.5*x**2.5 + 0.5*x**2.5 != 0)
problem_statement.Description: SymPy fails to combine/cancel algebraically-equal terms when the multiplicative subexpressions used as dict keys differ in internal Float representation (precision/mpf). The symptom is visible as two opposite terms with identical printed form (e.g. -0.5*x**2.5 + 0.5*x**2.5) instead of 0 after simplification.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Add.flatten (sympy/core/add.py, method Add.flatten): assumes structural dict-key equality is sufficient to detect like terms. It does c, s = o.as_coeff_Mul() and uses s directly as a dict key (terms[s] ...). When s contains Float exponents with different internal representation the same mathematical term becomes two different keys and coefficients are not combined.

2) Add.as_coefficients_dict (sympy/core/add.py, method Add.as_coefficients_dict): assumes the multiplicative part m returned by ai.as_coeff_Mul() can be used directly as a dict key and aggregates coefficients with d[m].append(c). If m objects are numerically-equal but structurally different, the same algebraic term is stored under multiple keys.

3) Mul._gather (sympy/core/mul.py, helper _gather inside Mul.flatten): keys exponent residues using co[1] (result of e.as_coeff_Mul()) via common_b.setdefault(b, {}).setdefault(co[1], []).append(co[0]). This relies on structural equality of co[1]; differing Float internals prevent grouping of identical powers and break subsequent power-collection logic.

4) Float._hashable_content (sympy/core/numbers.py, class Float): encodes structural identity as (self._mpf_, self._prec). Because the Hash/Basic structural identity includes the internal mpf tuple and precision (_mpf_, _prec), two Floats that are numerically equal (for example 2.5 produced by different routes or precisions) will hash/compare as different Basic objects. This is the mechanistic reason identical-looking exponents become distinct dict keys in Add/Mul grouping code.

5) Correct relationship required: grouping/aggregation of algebraic terms must treat subexpressions that are mathematically equal as identical keys (i.e., equality based on symbolic/numeric mathematical equality), not rely solely on Python/Basic structural hash/equality that can differ for Float objects with different internal precision/mpf.
StepsToReproduce: Install sympy and run a Python session with sympy imported.
Execute the following code snippet: import sympy; from sympy import *; x=Symbol('x'); expr1 = S(1)/2*x**2.5; expr2 = S(1)*x**(S(5)/2)/2; res = expr1 - expr2; res = simplify(res.evalf(5)); print(res)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The two opposite terms should combine/cancel and the simplified result should be 0 (no remaining terms).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The output displays two opposite terms that look identical when printed (for example '-0.5*x**2.5 + 0.5*x**2.5') instead of 0, indicating coefficients were not merged due to differing internal representation of subexpression keys.

problem_statement.Title: Mod.eval treats p = q**n as divisible for any integer n; Mod(x**2, x) simplifies incorrectly to 0 and blocks numeric remainder
problem_statement.Description: sympy.core.mod.Mod.eval contains a condition that treats p = q**n (where n is any Integer) as divisible by q. This causes expressions like Mod(x**2, x) to simplify symbolically to 0 even though, after substituting a non-integer numeric value for x (e.g. 1.5), the numeric remainder is non-zero (0.75). The simplification therefore survives substitution and yields an incorrect numeric result.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/mod.py::Mod.eval (doit helper) encodes the incorrect assumption: it uses the clause `p.is_Pow and p.exp.is_Integer and p.base == q` to decide that Mod(p,q) == 0. This assumes divisibility for any integer exponent n, which is false for n <= 0. The correct relationship for the divisibility shortcut is: require the exponent to be an integer AND positive (i.e. `p.is_Pow and p.base == q and p.exp.is_integer and p.exp.is_positive`).

2) sympy/core/function.py::Function.__new__ (Application.__new__ path) invokes `eval` during construction; because Mod.eval runs at construction time, the incorrect simplification is applied globally whenever Mod(...) objects are created.

3) sympy/core/basic.py::Basic.subs constructs new Mod instances via `self.func(*args)` during substitution; therefore a symbolic Mod simplified to 0 by Mod.eval will persist after substitution and block correct numeric evaluation (e.g. Mod(x**2, x).subs({x: 1.5}) remains 0 instead of producing 0.75).

4) sympy/core/tests/test_arit.py::test_Mod (existing tests) does not cover negative/zero exponent cases or the substitution-after-simplification scenario. Missing test coverage allowed this incorrect assumption to go unchecked.

5) The investigation did not inspect sympy.polys or other polynomial-specific modules for code that may have relied on the prior, broader simplification (polynomial-remainder semantics). This is a potential semantic/usage gap to review before changing behavior.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import symbols, S, Mod
Evaluate Mod(x**2, x).subs({x: S(1.5)}) -> currently yields 0 (incorrect); the numeric remainder for x=1.5 should be 0.75
Alternatively, set x = S(1.5) then evaluate Mod(x**2, x) -> yields 0.75 (numeric evaluation differs from the substituted symbolic path)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Mod(p, q) should simplify to 0 only when there is a provable divisibility relationship. In particular, when p is q**n, the simplification to 0 should apply only for integer n >= 1. User-visible examples: Mod(x**2, x) may remain as Mod(x**2, x) symbolically (not simplified to 0) unless the exponent is known positive; after substituting x = 1.5, Mod(x**2, x).subs({x: 1.5}) should evaluate to the numeric remainder 0.75.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Mod.eval treats p = q**n with any integer n as divisible by q. As a result Mod(x**2, x) is simplified to 0 symbolically. That symbolic 0 can persist through Basic.subs so that Mod(x**2, x).subs({x: 1.5}) returns 0 instead of the correct numeric remainder 0.75.

hints_text:
Even if `p.base` is an integer, the exponent must also be positive.

```
if (p == q or p == -q or p.is_integer and q == 1 or
        p.base == q and q.is_integer and p.is_Pow and p.exp.is_Integer
        and p.exp.is_positive):
    return S.Zero
```

because

```
>>> 2**-2 % S(2)
1/4
```
I would like to work on this. 
One would need just a slight change in the order of the properties, 


            p.is_Pow and p.base == q and q.is_integer and p.exp.is_Integer
            and p.exp.is_positive):
            return S.Zero

instead of

        p.base == q and q.is_integer and p.is_Pow and p.exp.is_Integer
        and p.exp.is_positive):
        return S.Zero


otherwise one gets an Attribute error:'Symbol' object has no attribute 'base' from
>>> Mod(x**2, x).

problem_statement.Title: bell(n).limit(n, oo) returns bell(oo) (unevaluated) instead of Infinity
problem_statement.Description: Calling limit(bell(n), n, oo) (or bell(n).limit(n, oo)) yields the unevaluated Function bell(oo) instead of the mathematical infinity (oo). The issue manifests when SymPy's limit heuristics reconstruct the function at the argument-limit and rely on the function class to simplify special inputs.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/functions/combinatorial/numbers.py::bell.eval is missing a special-case for n is S.Infinity. Current assumption: there is no canonical/simpler value for bell(S.Infinity), so bell(S.Infinity) remains unevaluated (bell(oo)). Correct relationship: bell(S.Infinity) should evaluate to S.Infinity (mathematical infinity).

2) sympy/series/limits.py heuristics reconstruct function values via rv = e.func(*r) where r is the list of per-argument limits. This code assumes function classes will simplify when called with argument-limits. Because bell.eval does not simplify bell(S.Infinity), the heuristics yield bell(oo) rather than S.Infinity.

3) sympy/functions/combinatorial/numbers.py::fibonacci.eval and ::lucas.eval implement the pattern "if n is S.Infinity: return S.Infinity". Those siblings show the intended/design precedent for combinatorial sequences; bell.eval deviates from this pattern and encodes the incorrect assumption that infinity need not be handled.

4) bell.eval currently only handles integer and polynomial input cases (it tests n.is_Integer and n.is_nonnegative) and therefore encodes the incorrect assumption that non-integer special inputs (like S.Infinity) have no canonical simplification. Location: sympy/functions/combinatorial/numbers.py::bell.eval.

5) The implementation omission also leaves related cases unverified: multi-argument forms (e.g. bell(S.Infinity, k_sym) for Bell polynomial variants) and negative infinity (bell(-oo)) were not explicitly handled or tested. Location of omission: sympy/functions/combinatorial/numbers.py::bell.eval and the repository's test suite (no concrete tests for bell limit-to-infinity were present in the investigation evidence).
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import symbols, bell, oo, limit
Evaluate bell(n).limit(n, oo) or limit(bell(n), n, oo)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The call limit(bell(n), n, oo) (or bell(n).limit(n, oo)) returns Infinity (S.Infinity / oo) in SymPy.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The call returns an unevaluated function application bell(oo) instead of Infinity.

problem_statement.Title: Python2->3 unpickle of Float fails due to Python-2 long suffix in mantissa string
problem_statement.Description: Unpickling a SymPy expression that was pickled under Python 2 and contains a Float raises a ValueError in Float.__new__ under Python 3 because the mantissa string contains a Python‑2 long suffix ('L'), which the code attempts to parse as a pure hexadecimal integer.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/numbers.py:Float.__new__ — Incorrect assumption that num[1] is exactly type str and contains only hex digits; code does type(num[1]) is str and then calls long(num[1], 16) directly. Concrete failing value: num[1] == '1L' (the Python‑2 long suffix), which makes long(..., 16) raise ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 16: '1L'.

2) sympy/core/numbers.py:Float.__new__ — Failure to accept other string-like inputs produced by pickling across Python versions: num[1] can be a bytes object or a Py2-style string with trailing 'L'/'l'; the code's strict type check (type(...) is str) and lack of sanitization are brittle across Py2->Py3 pickles.

3) sympy/core/numbers.py:Float.__getnewargs__ (writer/serialization side) — The method returns (mlib.to_pickable(self._mpf_),) so the picklable mantissa originates from mpmath.libmp; because mpmath is external and may emit Py2-style representations (including 'L'), SymPy's unpickle path must tolerate that output rather than rely on the producer being changed.

4) sympy/core/compatibility.py — Compatibility helpers (long and string_types) exist and indicate the intended cross-version handling; numbers.py does not use isinstance(num[1], string_types) and compatibility.long, which are the correct relationships for cross-Python parsing. The mantissa string should be treated as a string_types instance (or decoded from bytes) and sanitized (e.g., strip a trailing 'L'/'l') before parsing with the cross-version integer constructor (compatibility.long) using base 16.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following on a system with the reported SymPy version and Python 2 & 3 available:
python2 -c 'import pickle; import sympy; x = sympy.symbols("x"); print pickle.dumps(x + 1.0, 2)' | python3 -c 'import pickle; import sys; print(pickle.loads(sys.stdin.buffer.read()))'
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Loading a pickled SymPy expression that contains floats and that was pickled under Python 2 should succeed under Python 3 and reconstruct the same expression (for example, printing 'x + 1.0').
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Unpickling fails with a ValueError during Float construction: "ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 16: '1L'" (trace points to sympy/core/numbers.py line 1045), preventing the pickled expression from being loaded.

problem_statement.Title: NameError in coth.eval: references undefined 'cotm' instead of 'cothm'
problem_statement.Description: Calling subs on expressions like coth(log(tan(x))) (then substituting numeric integers) raises a NameError because coth.eval references an undefined variable 'cotm' in the arg.is_Add branch.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/functions/elementary/hyperbolic.py, class coth, method eval: the code computes cothm = coth(m) but tests the undefined name cotm (current code: 'if cotm is S.ComplexInfinity:'). This single-variable-name typo causes a NameError at runtime. The correct relationship is to test the computed variable 'cothm' (i.e. 'if cothm is S.ComplexInfinity:').

2) The bug is localized to that single instance in hyperbolic.py. The analogous, correct pattern exists in sympy/functions/elementary/trigonometric.py (cot.eval uses 'cotm = cot(m)' followed by 'if cotm is S.ComplexInfinity:'), demonstrating the intended naming convention and confirming the hyperbolic occurrence is a copy/typo error.

3) Because the undefined name is referenced in the arg.is_Add branch of coth.eval, any input that triggers that branch (e.g. coth(log(tan(x))) when substituting certain integer values) will raise the NameError 'name "cotm" is not defined' at runtime. Repository search shows no other occurrences that require the same change.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import Symbol, coth, log, tan
e.subs(x, 2)  # raises NameError: name 'cotm' is not defined (traceback into sympy/functions/elementary/hyperbolic.py)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Performing the substitution should not raise an exception; for the example above, e.subs(x, 2) should return the SymPy expression coth(log(tan(2))) (or an equivalent simplified expression) without a NameError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A runtime NameError is raised: "NameError: name 'cotm' is not defined" with traceback pointing to sympy/functions/elementary/hyperbolic.py (coth.eval, around line ~590) when the arg.is_Add branch is executed during substitution.

hints_text: There is a typo on [line 590](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/sympy/functions/elementary/hyperbolic.py#L590): `cotm` should be `cothm`.

problem_statement.Title: Matrix.col_insert shifts tail columns incorrectly (MatrixShaping._eval_col_insert arithmetic error)
problem_statement.Description: Inserting columns into a dense Matrix using Matrix.col_insert() maps columns after the inserted block to the wrong original columns, producing a mis-shifted tail (e.g., an identity block moves from the bottom rows to the top). The incorrect behavior occurs in the dense construction path used by Matrix.col_insert.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect tail-column index arithmetic in sympy/matrices/common.py :: MatrixShaping._eval_col_insert: the code computes the tail mapping as self[i, j - pos - other.cols]. This encodes the wrong assumption that the original column index must subtract both pos and other.cols. The correct relationship for columns after inserting other.cols columns at position pos is self[i, j - other.cols] (only shift back by the number of inserted columns).  
2) Matrix constructor convention (sympy/matrices/matrices.py :: Matrix._handle_creation_inputs): the dense constructor calls op(i, j) with i=row and j=col (row-major). This confirms entry(i,j) in MatrixShaping._eval_col_insert must treat i as row and j as column, so the incorrect arithmetic is specifically in the column dimension.  
3) Sparse sibling implementation is correct (sympy/matrices/sparse.py :: SparseMatrix._eval_col_insert): existing columns are shifted with "if col >= icol: col += other.cols" and other is inserted at offset. This corroborates that the intended dense semantics are to shift tail columns by other.cols only.  
4) Complementary deletion logic is consistent (sympy/matrices/common.py :: MatrixShaping._eval_col_del): single-column deletion maps tail columns via self[i, j + 1], showing the inverse relationship expected for a single-column insert; multi-column insert/delete behavior is consistent with repeated application.  
5) Localization of the defect: repository search found the wrong expression (j - pos - other.cols) only in sympy/matrices/common.py :: MatrixShaping._eval_col_insert, indicating the bug is a single-line logic error confined to the dense-shaping path.  
6) Investigation omissions: the analysis did not run the test suite, did not execute runtime examples beyond inspection, and did not inspect commit history to identify when the refactor introduced this arithmetic mistake.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import sympy as sm; M = sm.eye(6); V = 2 * sm.ones(6, 2); print(M.col_insert(3, V))"
In an interactive session: import sympy as sm; M = sm.eye(6); V = 2*sm.ones(6,2); then call M.col_insert(3, V) and inspect the resulting matrix
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The inserted block of columns appears at the specified insertion position, and all original columns that were after that position are shifted right by exactly the number of columns inserted (other.cols), preserving their relative order and row alignment.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Columns after the inserted block are drawn from incorrect original columns (an extra shift occurs), so blocks that should remain aligned with the bottom rows appear shifted to the top (e.g., the right-hand identity block ends up in the top rows instead of the bottom).

hints_text: It seems that `pos` shouldn't be [here](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/sympy/matrices/common.py#L89).

problem_statement.Title: __matmul__ (@) accepts scalars by delegating to __mul__, allowing 2 @ Matrix(...)
problem_statement.Description: The matrix multiplication operator (@ / __matmul__) on concrete Matrix objects accepts scalar operands and performs scalar (elementwise) multiplication because __matmul__ and __rmatmul__ unconditionally delegate to __mul__/__rmul__ instead of requiring matrix-like operands. This manifests when using a scalar with @ (e.g. 2 @ Matrix(...)).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption encoded in sympy/matrices/common.py:MatrixArithmetic.__matmul__: __matmul__ unconditionally returns self.__mul__(other), encoding the assumption that '@' should behave exactly like '*' for concrete Matrix objects. This forwarding allows the scalar branch in __mul__ to run when 'other' is a non-iterable scalar.

2. Incorrect assumption encoded in sympy/matrices/common.py:MatrixArithmetic.__rmatmul__: __rmatmul__ unconditionally returns self.__rmul__(other), encoding the symmetric assumption for the reverse operation; __rmul__ contains a scalar branch, so scalar @ Matrix succeeds via this path.

3. Behavior of the called methods (sympy/matrices/common.py:MatrixArithmetic.__mul__ and __rmul__): these methods implement both matrix-matrix and scalar-matrix semantics by using _matrixify and branching on other.is_Matrix / other.is_MatrixLike / non-iterable scalar. The unconditional delegation from __matmul__/__rmatmul__ to these methods therefore permits scalar operands for '@'. (The incorrect assumption is not inside __mul__/__rmul__, but in the unconditional forwarding.)

4. Scope note (sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py:MatrixExpr.__matmul__): symbolic MatrixExpr matmul is implemented separately (mapping to MatMul) and is not affected; the defect is confined to concrete MatrixArithmetic.__matmul__/__rmatmul__.

5. Missing regression protection: no agent-produced git patch or unit tests were found; tests asserting that scalar @ Matrix raises an error (and matrix @ matrix still works) are needed to prevent regression (investigation gap).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Matrix @ Matrix performs linear algebra matrix multiplication. Using a scalar with @ (either scalar @ Matrix or Matrix @ scalar) should be rejected and raise an error indicating scalar operands are not allowed for matrix multiplication (and direct the user to use '*' for scalar multiplication).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Concrete Matrix.__matmul__ and __rmatmul__ currently accept scalar operands and perform elementwise scalar multiplication (e.g. 2 @ Matrix(...) returns the same result as 2 * Matrix(...)) instead of raising an error.

hints_text:
Note to anyone fixing this: `@`/`__matmul__` only works in Python 3.5+. 
I would like to work on this issue.

problem_statement.Title: simplify() extracts numeric factor from power with symbolic exponent, producing inequivalent (complex) result
problem_statement.Description: Calling simplify() on expressions like (-x/4 - 1/12)**x - 1 causes the simplifier to factor a numeric denominator out of the power (producing 12**(-x)*(...)**x). That transformation is not semantics-preserving for general symbolic exponents and turns a real result into a complex one for x = 9/5.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Unsafe algebraic assumption encoded in power extraction: the code effectively assumes (a*b)**x == a**x * b**x for general (symbolic, non-integer) exponents x. This assumption is incorrect because principal-root/branch choices depend on the exponent and sign of factors, and extracting numeric content can change real values to complex ones. (Location: conceptual/semantic).  

2) Pow.as_content_primitive unguarded extraction: sympy/core/power.py::Pow.as_content_primitive currently allows extracting a positive rational 'content' R from a power without proving the extraction is safe for the exponent. The method returns (R, self/R) for non-trivial R even when self.exp is a general symbolic exponent and the base may be non-positive. This extraction is the direct source of the 12**(-x) factor in the reported example. (Location: sympy/core/power.py::Pow.as_content_primitive).  

3) simplify() materializes the extracted content without re-checking safety: sympy/simplify/simplify.py::simplify calls expr = Mul(*powsimp(expr).as_content_primitive()), so any content returned by Pow.as_content_primitive is immediately made explicit at top level. Because as_content_primitive extracted 12**(-x), simplify produced an inequivalent expression. (Location: sympy/simplify/simplify.py::simplify).  

4) powsimp / Mul._eval_power are enabling collaborators: sympy/simplify/powsimp.py::powsimp and sympy/core/mul.py::Mul._eval_power (and Pow._eval_expand_power_base) create/reshape expressions in ways that allow as_content_primitive to perform the extraction. These code paths can therefore precipitate the unsafe transform in multiple places. (Locations: sympy/simplify/powsimp.py::powsimp; sympy/core/mul.py::Mul._eval_power; sympy/core/power.py::Pow._eval_expand_power_base).  

5) Concrete semantic divergence demonstrated by numeric example: for e = (-x/4 - 1/12)**x - 1 and f = simplify(e) the simplifier produces f = 12**(-x)*(-12**x + (-3*x - 1)**x). Substituting x = 9/5 yields: simplify(e.subs(x,9/5)) -> -1 - 32*15**(1/5)*2**(2/5)/225 (numeric N = -1.32255049319339, real), while simplify(f.subs(x,9/5)) -> -1 - 32*(-1)**(4/5)*60**(1/5)/225 (numeric N = -0.739051169462523 - 0.189590423018741*I, complex). This proves the extraction changes semantics.  

6) Correct extraction invariant (diagnostic): numeric content R should only be extracted from a power (i.e., return a non-trivial (R, self/R)) when the extraction is provably semantics-preserving for the exponent — at minimum this requires either that the exponent is an integer (self.exp.is_integer is True) or that the base is provably positive (self.base.is_positive is True). If neither condition holds, the safe relationship is to treat the power as having no extractable numeric content (i.e., return (1, self)). (Location where invariant must hold: sympy/core/power.py::Pow.as_content_primitive).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: simplify() should not produce algebraic transformations that change the numeric value of an expression for legitimate numeric substitutions. For the example (-x/4 - 1/12)**x - 1, simplify() should produce an expression that is semantically equivalent to the original for symbolic x; substituting x = 9/5 and evaluating numerically should yield the same real value (-1.32255049319339...).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: simplify() returns 12**(-x)*(-12**x + (-3*x - 1)**x) for (-x/4 - 1/12)**x - 1, and after substituting x = 9/5 the simplified form evaluates to a different (complex) numeric result (approx. -0.739051169462523 - 0.189590423018741*I) while the original expression evaluates to a real number (approx. -1.32255049319339).

hints_text:
The expressions really are equivalent, `simplify` is not to blame.  SymPy is inconsistent when raising negative numbers to the power of 9/5 (and probably other rational powers). 
```
>>> (-S(1))**(S(9)/5)
-(-1)**(4/5)                  #  complex number as a result 
>>> (-S(4))**(S(9)/5)
-8*2**(3/5)                  # the result is real
```
In a way, both are reasonable. The computation starts by writing 9/5 as 1 + 4/5. Then we get the base factored out, and are left with `(-1)**(4/5)` or `(-4)**(4/5)`. Somehow, the first is left alone while in the second, noticing that 4 is a square, SymPy does further manipulations, ending up by raising (-4) to the power of 4 and thus canceling the minus sign. So we get the second result.  

Can it be accepted that the expression is multi-valued and which of the possible values is chosen is arbitrary? But one perhaps would like more consistency on this.
OK, "inequivalent" was the wrong word. But is it reasonable to expect sympy to try to keep the same complex root choice through simplification?
Yes, I think there should be consistency there.  The issue is at the level of SymPy taking in an object like (-1)**(S(4)/5) and parsing it into an expression tree. The trees are formed in significantly different ways for different exponents: 
```
>>> for k in range(1, 5):
...     srepr((-4)**(S(k)/5))
'Pow(Integer(-4), Rational(1, 5))'    #  complex
'Pow(Integer(-4), Rational(2, 5))'    # complex 
'Mul(Integer(2), Pow(Integer(-2), Rational(1, 5)))'   # complex, factoring out 2 is okay
'Mul(Integer(2), Pow(Integer(2), Rational(3, 5)))'    # real, where did the minus sign go? 
```

problem_statement.Title: Incorrect cancellation of ComplexInfinity (zoo) causes r.subs(b, a) to return 1
problem_statement.Description: Substituting b -> a into r = (1/(a+b) + 1/(a-b)) / (1/(a+b) - 1/(a-b)) yields a finite value 1 in SymPy 1.1.1 even though subexpressions become undefined (involve ComplexInfinity). The substitution path performs a structural cancellation that incorrectly treats identical ComplexInfinity factors as 1.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Unconditional cancellation assumption in sympy/core/mul.py:Mul._combine_inverse — the method contains an early branch that returns S.One whenever lhs == rhs. This encodes the assumption that identical multiplicative factors always cancel to 1. Location: sympy/core/mul.py, class Mul, method _combine_inverse.

2) The unconditional lhs==rhs -> S.One branch contradicts ComplexInfinity arithmetic semantics: zoo/zoo is indeterminate (S.NaN). SymPy's numbers implementation and tests expect zoo/zoo -> S.NaN (sympy/core/numbers.py and sympy/core/tests/test_numbers.py contain/expect this behavior). Location: sympy/core/numbers.py (S.ComplexInfinity alias zoo) and test suite.

3) As a result of (1) and (2), substitution/matching machinery that relies on _combine_inverse (via sympy/core/operations.py:AssocOp._matches_commutative and sympy/core/basic.py:Basic.subs) can collapse ComplexInfinity/ComplexInfinity to S.One, producing an incorrect finite result for expressions that are structurally undefined. Location: sympy/core/operations.py:AssocOp._matches_commutative and sympy/core/basic.py:Basic.subs (the substitution/reconstruction flow).

4) Concrete incorrect hardcoded behavior: current code returns S.One for lhs == rhs. For the specific case where lhs is S.ComplexInfinity (zoo), the correct diagnostic value is S.NaN (i.e., zoo/zoo -> S.NaN). The unconditional equality branch should not treat zoo/zoo as 1; the expected relationship is zoo/zoo == S.NaN (sympy/core/tests/test_numbers.py asserts this).
StepsToReproduce: r.subs(b, a)  # in SymPy 1.1.1 this returns 1
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Substituting b -> a should not yield the finite value 1. Because subexpressions become undefined (involving ComplexInfinity), the substitution result should reflect that indeterminacy (e.g., produce an undefined/indeterminate result such as NaN or an expression containing ComplexInfinity/NaN). The limit as b -> a can still be -1, but the direct substitution must not produce a misleading finite value.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In SymPy 1.1.1, r.subs(b, a) returns 1 due to a structural cancellation. r.limit(b, a) returns -1. The substitution path collapses identical ComplexInfinity factors to 1, producing an incorrect finite result instead of indicating indeterminacy.

hints_text:
In this regard, don't you think that `r.simplify()` is wrong? It returns `-a/b` which is not correct if b=a.
`simplify` works for the generic case. SymPy would be hard to use if getting a+b from `simplify((a**2-b**2)/(a-b))` required an explicit declaration that a is not equal to b. (Besides, there is currently no way to express that declaration to `simplify`, anyway). This is part of reason we avoid `simplify` in code:  it can change the outcome in edge cases. 

The fundamental issue here is: for what kind of expression `expr` do we want expr/expr to return 1? Current behavior:

zoo / zoo   # nan
(zoo + 3) / (zoo + 3)   # nan
(zoo + a) / (zoo + a)    # 1  
(zoo + a) / (a - zoo)   # 1 because -zoo is zoo  (zoo is complex infinity)  

The rules for combining an expression with its inverse in Mul appear to be too lax. 

There is a check of the form `if something is S.ComplexInfinity`... which returns nan in the first two cases, but this condition is not met by `zoo + a`. 

But using something like `numerator.is_finite` would not work either, because most of the time, we don't know if a symbolic expression is finite. E.g., `(a+b).is_finite` is None, unknown,  unless the symbols were explicitly declared to be finite.

My best idea so far is to have three cases for expr/expr: 

1. expr is infinite or 0: return nan
2. Otherwise, if expr contains infinities (how to check this efficiently? Mul needs to be really fast), return expr/expr without combining 
3. Otherwise, return 1
"But using something like numerator.is_finite would not work either"

I had thought of something like denom.is_zero. If in expr_1/expr_2 the denominator is zero, the fraction is undefined. The only way to get a value from this is to use limits. At least i would think so.

My first idea was that sympy first simplifies and then substitutes. But then, the result should be -1. 

(zoo+a)/(a-zoo) # 1
explains what happens, but i had expected, that
zoo/expr leads to nan and expr/zoo leads to nan as well.

I agree, that Mul needs to be really fast, but this is about subst. But i confess, i don't know much about symbolic math.
zoo/3 is zoo, and 4/zoo is 0. I think it's convenient, and not controversial, to have these. 

Substitution is not to blame: it replaces b by a as requested, evaluating 1/(a-a) as zoo.  This is how `r` becomes `(1/(2*a) + zoo) / (1/(2*a) - zoo)`. So far nothing wrong has happened. The problem is that (because of -zoo being same as zoo) both parts are identified as the same and then the `_gather` helper of Mul method combines the powers 1 and -1 into power 0. And anything to power 0 returns 1 in SymPy, hence the result. 

I think we should prevent combining powers when base contains Infinity or ComplexInfinity. For example, (x+zoo) / (x+zoo)**2  returning 1 / (x+zoo) isn't right either. 
I dont really understand what happens. How can i get the result zoo? 

In my example `r.subs(b,a)` returns ` 1`,  
but `r.subs(b,-a)` returns `(zoo + 1/(2*a))/(zoo - 1/(2*a))`

So how is zoo defined? Is it `(1/z).limit(z,0)`? I get `oo` as result, but how is this related to  `zoo`? As far as i know, `zoo` is ComplexInfinity. By playing around, i just found another confusing result:

`(zoo+z)/(zoo-z)` returns `(z + zoo)/(-z + zoo)`, 
but
`(z + zoo)/(z-zoo)` returns 1

I just found, `1/S.Zero` returns `zoo`, as well as `(1/S.Zero)**2`. To me, that would mean i should not divide by `zoo`.
There are three infinities: positive infinity oo, negative infinity -oo, and complex infinity zoo. Here is the difference:

- If z is a positive number that tends to zero, then 1/z tends to oo
- if z is a negative number than tends to zero, then 1/z tends to -oo
- If z is a complex number that tends to zero, then 1/z tends to zoo

The complex infinity zoo does not have a determined sign, so -zoo is taken to  be the same as zoo. So when you put `(z + zoo)/(z-zoo)` two things happen: first, z-zoo returns z+zoo (you can check this directly) and second, the two identical expressions are cancelled, leaving 1.

However, in (zoo+z)/(zoo-z) the terms are not identical, so they do not cancel. 

I am considering a solution that returns NaN when Mul cancels an expression with infinity of any kind. So for example (z+zoo)/(z+zoo) and (z-oo)/(z-oo) both return NaN. However, it changes the behavior in a couple of tests, so I have to investigate whether the tests are being wrong about infinities, or something else is. 
Ok. I think i got it. Thank you for your patient explanation. 
Maybe one last question. Should `z + zoo` result in `zoo`? I think that would be natural.

problem_statement.Title: SeqFormula LaTeX printer emits '\left\[' / '\right\]' (escaped brackets) causing Markdown/Jupyter render problems
problem_statement.Description: SeqFormula's LaTeX output contains backslash-escaped square brackets (the literal tokens "\[" and "\]") instead of the correct "\left[" and "\right]", which causes copy/paste into Markdown/Jupyter to misrender the sequence.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect delimiter escaping in the LaTeX printer: LatexPrinter._print_SeqFormula (sympy/printing/latex.py) constructs the strings r"\left\[" and r"\right\]" (i.e. emits the literal tokens "\\[" and "\\]") — this encodes the wrong assumption that '[' must be escaped after \left/\right. The correct delimiters are r"\left[" and r"\right]". Emitting "\\["/"\\]" is incorrect because TeX interprets "\\["/"\\]" as display-math delimiters and this breaks downstream Markdown/Jupyter rendering.

2. Tests encode the buggy behavior: sympy/printing/tests/test_latex.py::test_latex_sequences contains assertions that expect strings like r'\left\[0, 1, 4, 9, \ldots\right\]'. Those tests therefore codify the current incorrect output and will need their expected strings updated if the printer is corrected.

3. Printing architecture location: Sequence-like objects are printed by the printer (not by SeqFormula itself); the producer of the wrong string is LatexPrinter._print_SeqFormula (sympy/printing/latex.py). The PrettyPrinter sibling (sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py::_print_SeqFormula) already produces the list form correctly, indicating the defect is localized to the LaTeX printer method.

4. Exact hardcoded values to change (evidence from code): r"\left\["  -->  r"\left[" and r"\right\]"  -->  r"\right]". Example expected-string edits in tests: r'\left\[0, 1, 4, 9, \ldots\right\]'  ->  r'\left[0, 1, 4, 9, \ldots\right]'.

5. Investigation gaps to validate before merging: the agent did not run an interactive Jupyter render to capture live output and did not exhaustively grep the repository for other occurrences of the problematic token sequence (patterns like "\\left\\\[" and "\\right\\\]"). These checks are outstanding and may reveal additional locations/tests that assert or produce the same incorrect tokens.
StepsToReproduce: In a Jupyter notebook run: import sympy as sp; k, m, n = sp.symbols('k m n', integer=True); sp.init_printing()
Observe the printed LaTeX string (it will contain the literal tokens '\\left\\[ ... \\right\\]')
Copy that printed string into a Markdown cell and render — the sequence will not render properly due to the '\\[' / '\\]' tokens.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The LaTeX printer should emit square-bracket delimiters without an extra backslash: use '\\left[' and '\\right]' so that a printed sequence appears as e.g. '\\left[0, 1, 4, 9, \\ldots\\right]'. Copying this into a Markdown cell should render the sequence correctly.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The LaTeX printer emits backslash-escaped square brackets (the string contains '\\left\\[0, 1, 4, 9, \\ldots\\right\\]'), and copying that output into a Markdown cell misrenders (because '\\[' and '\\]' are interpreted as display-math delimiters).

+    assert abs(Pow(Rational(-2, 3), Rational(-7, 4)).n() -
+        Pow(Rational(-2, 3), Rational(-7, 4), evaluate=False).n()) < 1e-16
 
     # negative integer power and negative rational base
     assert Rational(-2, 3) ** Rational(-2, 1) == Rational(9, 4)

problem_statement.Title: Numeric inconsistency evaluating simplify((-a)**x * a**(-x)) for symbolic vs numeric a
problem_statement.Description: Simplifying (-a)**x * a**(-x) yields (-1)**x, but numeric evaluation of the original and simplified expressions produces different complex results when a is a concrete negative-real number (e.g. a = 2) versus when a is a symbolic positive integer. The discrepancy stems from different evalf code paths for negative-real numeric bases.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/power.py :: Pow._eval_evalf — incorrect conditional that restricts the "invert base & flip exponent" rewrite to non-real numeric bases only. The code applies the rewrite only when: exp.is_negative and base.is_number and base.is_real is False. This excludes negative real numeric bases (for example base = -2), so negative-exponent powers with negative real numeric bases are not rewritten and follow a different numeric path.  

2) Correct relationship that was intended but not applied to negative real bases: when exp.is_negative and base.is_number and (base.is_real is False or base.is_negative) the code should invert the base and flip the exponent: base := base.conjugate() / (base * base.conjugate())._evalf(prec) (this expression computes 1/base both for complex bases and for negative real bases because conjugate(base)==base for real base and the denominator is positive real), then exp := -exp, and return Pow(base, exp). The missing base.is_negative guard is the concrete incorrect assumption.  

3) Supporting (non-buggy) implementation that enables the rewrite: sympy/core/numbers.py :: Float._eval_power already implements numeric mpf_pow with an mpc_pow fallback for complex results. That means performing the rewrite to produce a positive exponent on a (possibly complex) base will be handled consistently by Float._eval_power. This is not a bug but explains why changing the Pow._eval_evalf guard is safe.  

4) Investigation/testing gap: the repository trace for the concrete numeric path (Pow.__new__ -> Float._eval_power for the S(2) case across precisions) was not executed and unit tests covering the exact repro examples are missing. Add tests that assert numeric equality for the exact examples to prevent regression.
StepsToReproduce:
1) In a SymPy interactive session, run:
   a = Symbol('a', integer=True, positive=True)
   e = (-a)**x * a**(-x)
   f = simplify(e)
   print(e)
   print(f)
   t = -S(10)/3
   n1 = e.subs(x,t)
   n2 = f.subs(x,t)
   print(N(n1))
   print(N(n2))

2) Then run the concrete-base case:
   a = S(2)
   e = (-a)**x * a**(-x)
   f = simplify(e)
   print(e)
   print(f)
   t = -S(10)/3
   n1 = e.subs(x,t)
   n2 = f.subs(x,t)
   print(N(n1))
   print(N(n2))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For any substitution of numeric values for a and x, numeric evaluation of the original expression (-a)**x * a**(-x) and the simplified expression produced by simplify(...) should produce the same numeric result. In particular, for the repro example with x = -10/3 and a = 2, N(e.subs(x,t)) and N(f.subs(x,t)) should match.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Case 1 (symbolic a):
- a = Symbol('a', integer=True, positive=True)
- simplify((-a)**x * a**(-x)) yields (-1)**x
- substituting x = -10/3 and evaluating numerically gives the same complex number for original and simplified: -0.5 + 0.866025403784439*I

Case 2 (concrete a):
- a = S(2)
- simplify((-2)**x * 2**(-x)) yields (-1)**x
- substituting x = -10/3 and evaluating numerically gives different results:
  - original expression numeric: 0.5 - 0.866025403784439*I
  - simplified expression numeric: -0.5 + 0.866025403784439*I
These differing numeric results demonstrate the evalf asymmetry for negative-real numeric bases.

hints_text:
More succinctly, the problem is
```
>>> (-2)**(-S(10)/3)
-(-2)**(2/3)/16
```
Pow is supposed to use the principal branch, which means (-2) has complex argument pi, which under exponentiation becomes `-10*pi/3` or equivalently `2*pi/3`. But the result of automatic simplification is different: its argument is -pi/3. 

The base (-1) is handled correctly, though.
```
>>> (-1)**(-S(10)/3)
(-1)**(2/3)
```
Hence the inconsistency, because the simplified form of the product has (-1) in its base.

problem_statement.Title: Pretty-printer duplicates/mispositions basis-vector name for multi-line scalar coefficients
problem_statement.Description: When a vector is pretty-printed with a scalar coefficient whose prettyForm is multi-line (for example (x/y)**t * e.j), the basis-vector name is duplicated on each line of the coefficient and the vertical baseline/centering is incorrect.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py:PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent — The printer treats prettyForm output as raw strings and performs substring/newline edits to reposition the basis name. It builds strings like arg_str + ' ' + k._pretty_form, then uses replace/remove operations (e.g. tempstr = tempstr.replace(vectstrs[i], '')). This assumption is false for multi-line prettyForms and directly causes the duplicated basis name and misalignment.

2) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py:PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent — The method relies on brittle, hardcoded layout operations, including replacing the Unicode RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK with a version followed by the basis text and numeric spacing constants. Explicit examples observed: use of the replacement involving the RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK, and numeric padding expressions such as 3*(len(lengths)-1) and lengths[-1] + 3. These ad-hoc constants/edits encode incorrect fixed-spacing assumptions that fail for complex/multi-line prettyForms.

3) sympy/vector/vector.py:BaseVector._pretty_form and sympy/vector/scalar.py:BaseScalar._pretty — The codebase inconsistently exposes pretty output: BaseScalar provides a _pretty method returning a prettyForm object, while BaseVector stores only a _pretty_form string and lacks a _pretty method. PrettyPrinter directly accesses k._pretty_form (raw string) for vectors. The incorrect assumption is that vector _pretty_form strings are safe to concatenate/manipulate like raw text; in reality the printing API provides structured primitives (prettyForm/stringPict) that preserve baselines and multi-line layout.

4) sympy/vector/dyadic.py — The dyadic implementation constructs obj._pretty_form via raw-string concatenation: '(' + vector1._pretty_form + '|' + vector2._pretty_form + ')'. This encodes the same wrong assumption (that pretty output is raw text) and indicates other vector-related pretty paths share the brittle approach.

5) sympy/printing/pretty/stringpict.py — The investigation shows correct primitives exist (prettyForm, stringPict.next, prettyForm.parens) that preserve baseline and multi-line alignment. Their presence demonstrates that the printer's raw-string surgical edits are unnecessary and the wrong abstraction is being used in the locations above.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL with sympy available.
Evaluate (x/y)**t * e.j and observe the pretty-printed output
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The scalar coefficient, even if rendered as a multi-line prettyForm, should appear as a single multi-line block with the basis vector name displayed once to the right of that block and vertically centered (baseline aligned) relative to the coefficient.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The basis-vector name (e_j) appears duplicated on each line of the multi-line scalar prettyForm, and the overall baseline/centering of the term is incorrect (the basis name is not vertically centered relative to the coefficient).
