problem_statement.Title: nthroot_mod omits trivial root x ≡ 0 (mod p) when a % p == 0
problem_statement.Description: When solving x**n ≡ a (mod p) with a ≡ 0 (mod p), nthroot_mod fails to include the trivial solution x ≡ 0 (mod p). Example: nthroot_mod(17*17, 5, 17) should include the root 0 but does not.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/ntheory/residue_ntheory.py:nthroot_mod assumes public entry can be handled by multiplicative-group logic and lacks a short-circuit for a % p == 0. Location: nthroot_mod. Consequence: the trivial root x ≡ 0 is never returned when a % p == 0. Correct diagnostic behavior: when a % p == 0, return 0 (or [0] when all_roots=True).

2) sympy/ntheory/residue_ntheory.py:_nthroot_mod1 assumes its input s is a unit (s % p != 0) and immediately computes primitive_root(p) / discrete_log on values derived from s. Location: _nthroot_mod1. Consequence: if s % p == 0 then s1 == 0 and discrete_log is invoked with 0 (an invalid multiplicative-group input), leading to omission/failure. Correct relationship: reduce s mod p then if s == 0 return 0 (or [0] when all_roots=True).

3) sympy/ntheory/residue_ntheory.py:_nthroot_mod2 and callers rely on _nthroot_mod1's contract. Location: _nthroot_mod2 and internal callers. Consequence: without a guard in _nthroot_mod1, internal code paths can propagate the missing-zero behavior even if the public API is guarded.

4) Consumer inconsistency: is_nthpow_residue and sqrt_mod already treat a % p == 0 as a valid case (they return True / 0 respectively). Location: is_nthpow_residue, sqrt_mod. Consequence: nthroot_mod's omission of 0 is inconsistent with related APIs and breaks callers that expect the complete residue set (e.g., solveset._invert_modular).

5) Test-suite gap: tests do not assert the contract for a % p == 0. Location: sympy/ntheory/tests/test_residue.py. Consequence: the regression was not caught by existing tests. Concrete assertions expected: nthroot_mod(17*17, 5, 17) == 0 and nthroot_mod(17*17, 5, 17, True) == [0].
StepsToReproduce: In a Python REPL or test file, from sympy.ntheory.residue_ntheory import nthroot_mod
Call nthroot_mod(17*17, 5, 17) and observe the returned value does not include 0
Call nthroot_mod(17*17, 5, 17, True) and observe the returned list does not contain [0]
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a % p == 0, nthroot_mod should include the trivial solution x ≡ 0 (mod p). For the public API: nthroot_mod(a, n, p) should return 0 and nthroot_mod(a, n, p, all_roots=True) should return [0]. More generally, the set of returned roots for x**n ≡ a (mod p) must include 0 whenever a ≡ 0 (mod p).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: nthroot_mod does not include 0 when a % p == 0; it proceeds into multiplicative-group code paths and omits the trivial root. Example: nthroot_mod(289, 5, 17) fails to return 0 (or [0] when all_roots=True).

problem_statement.Title: expr.atoms() (no-arg) returns Atom subclasses with args instead of true leaf nodes
problem_statement.Description: Calling expr.atoms() with no arguments returns instances of Atom subclasses (isinstance(..., Atom)) even when those objects have non-empty .args. The method therefore does not return expression-tree leaves (objects whose .args == ()). This manifests in Basic.atoms and is reproduced at the Matrix level.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/basic.py::Basic.atoms encodes the incorrect assumption "Atom subclasses == leaf nodes" by using the literal default types = (Atom,) when no types are supplied. The method filters nodes with isinstance(expr, types), which includes Atom subclasses that expose .args. The correct selection for the no-types case is: iterate preorder_traversal(self) and add expr when not getattr(expr, 'args', ()). (Preserve variable names: types, Atom, preorder_traversal, expr.args.)

2) sympy/matrices/common.py::Matrix.atoms forces the same wrong default by setting types = (Atom,) when called without types and then calling self._eval_atoms(*types). This propagates the isinstance-based Atom filter to matrix elements even if Basic.atoms is changed. The correct relationship is: only convert and pass types when types is truthy; otherwise call self._eval_atoms() so element-level atoms() uses the leaf-based default.

3) The class-level flag is_Atom and the Atom subclass hierarchy are not a reliable definition of leaves: some classes set is_Atom = True yet have non-empty .args. Example evidence: sympy/assumptions/assume.py::AppliedPredicate sets is_Atom = True but represents applied predicates and carries arguments. Therefore isinstance(obj, Atom) does not imply obj.args == ().

4) The explicit-type branch must remain unchanged: calls of the form atoms(TypeA, TypeB, ...) must continue to collect instances by type. Only the no-argument (default) branch is incorrect and must select nodes with no .args.

5) The incorrect literal default to change is exactly the tuple assignment types = (Atom,) found in two locations: sympy/core/basic.py and sympy/matrices/common.py. A repo search returned only these two literal occurrences, limiting the scope of the change.

6) Exact selection logic that preserves current semantics for explicit types but changes the no-types behavior (as discovered in the investigation):
   - If types is truthy: convert entries with t if isinstance(t, type) else type(t); for expr in preorder_traversal(self): if isinstance(expr, types): result.add(expr).
   - Else (no types): for expr in preorder_traversal(self): if not getattr(expr, 'args', ()): result.add(expr).

7) Missing runtime verification: the investigation did not execute concrete REPL examples or test-suite/performance checks. These verification steps remain outstanding and are necessary to confirm behavioral and perf effects.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python interpreter with the sympy package from the repository.
Construct or obtain an expression whose class is marked as an Atom subclass but has non-empty .args (for example, an AppliedPredicate or any class that sets is_Atom = True and exposes arguments).
Observe that the returned set includes the Atom-subclass instance(s) that have non-empty .args (i.e., non-leaf objects are present).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: expr.atoms() called with no arguments returns only leaf subexpressions — i.e., objects in the expression tree whose .args attribute is empty (expr.args == ()). The explicit-type form atoms(TypeA, ...) continues to return instances matching the given types.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: expr.atoms() returns Atom-subclass instances based on isinstance(..., Atom) even when those instances have non-empty .args, so non-leaf objects (Atom subclasses with arguments) appear in the result.

I think you're misunderstanding the issue. The issue is not to remove .args. Indeed, every SymPy object should have .args in order to be valid.

problem_statement.Title: Converting a single-block BlockDiagMatrix to Matrix raises TypeError ('One' object is not subscriptable)
problem_statement.Description: When a BlockDiagMatrix is constructed with exactly one Matrix element and then converted to a concrete Matrix (Matrix(BlockDiagMatrix(M))), a TypeError is raised because the block container was flattened into a numeric matrix instead of preserved as a 1×1 matrix-of-blocks.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in sympy/matrices/expressions/blockmatrix.py:BlockDiagMatrix.blocks: the code builds data = [[mats[i] if i == j else ZeroMatrix(...) ...]] and returns ImmutableDenseMatrix(data) assuming ImmutableDenseMatrix will preserve nested Matrix objects as single block elements. This is false for the default evaluate=True case; ImmutableDenseMatrix(data) will expand/concatenate nested Matrix objects so [[M]] -> M.  
2) Incorrect assumption in callers of ImmutableDenseMatrix (sympy/matrices/matrices.py / immutable.py: ImmutableDenseMatrix._new and _handle_creation_inputs): callers may omit evaluate=False expecting a matrix-of-blocks, but when evaluate=True the constructor actively flattens nested Matrix objects by concatenating submatrices.  
3) Symptom location: sympy/matrices/expressions/blockmatrix.py:BlockMatrix._entry assumes self.blocks[row_block, col_block] is a Matrix-like block supporting [i, j]; because BlockDiagMatrix.blocks let ImmutableDenseMatrix expand the inner Matrix, self.blocks[0,0] can be a scalar (S.One) or numeric entry instead, causing the TypeError: 'One' object is not subscriptable.  
4) Correct relationship/constant (diagnostic): ImmutableDenseMatrix(..., evaluate=True) (the current default) causes concatenation/flattening of nested Matrix objects; to preserve nested Matrix objects as elements of a matrix-of-blocks the constructor must be invoked with evaluate=False. (This relationship is evidenced by the existing correct pattern in BlockMatrix.__new__, which uses ImmutableDenseMatrix(rows, evaluate=False)).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Converting a BlockDiagMatrix containing a single Matrix block to an explicit Matrix should produce the same Matrix M (Matrix(BlockDiagMatrix(M)) == M) without raising exceptions.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Matrix(BlockDiagMatrix(M)) raises TypeError: 'One' object is not subscriptable (the conversion path attempts to subscript a scalar because the block container was flattened instead of preserving the inner Matrix as a block).

problem_statement.Title: sqf_list returns different square-free factorization for product vs expanded polynomial
problem_statement.Description: Calling sqf_list on a product of multiplicands can return separate linear factors for roots that should be grouped into a single higher-degree factor (and reported with the correct multiplicity). The inconsistency appears when the input is a Mul of sub-expressions vs a single Poly/expanded expression.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption encoded in sympy/polys/polytools.py::_symbolic_factor_list: the function assumes factoring each multiplicative sub-argument separately (iterating over Mul.make_args(expr)) and concatenating their factor lists yields the same canonical square-free decomposition as factoring the entire polynomial. This prevents detection of factors that span multiplicands (example consequence: (x - 2)*(x - 3) is left as separate factors (x - 2, 1) and (x - 3, 1) rather than the grouped factor (x**2 - 5*x + 6, 1) when multiplicity > 1). The repro shows multiplicity 3 should be applied to the quadratic: expected (x**2 - 5*x + 6, 3).

2. Implementation detail in sympy/polys/polytools.py::_symbolic_factor_list that encodes the bug: the function never attempts to convert the whole expression to a Poly via _poly_from_expr(expr, opt) before factoring; instead it calls _poly_from_expr on each multiplicand/base and extends factors with each multiplicand's factor list. This missing attempt to 'polify' the entire expression is the immediate cause of the inconsistent output.

3. Incorrect assumption in the public-wrapper flow that per-multiplicand symbolic factoring is acceptable for all callers: sympy/polys/polytools.py:_generic_factor_list (and public sqf_list/factor_list wrappers) rely on _symbolic_factor_list. Because _symbolic_factor_list uses per-multiplicand factoring, public APIs produce results that differ from Poly.sqf_list (sympy/polys/polyclasses.py:Poly.sqf_list) which delegates to low-level dmp/dup_sqf_list and does produce the canonical grouped factors.

4. Implicit assumption about ordering/format compatibility encoded around sympy/polys/polytools.py:_sorted_factors: the code assumes downstream sorting/formatting will keep results compatible with existing callers. After grouping via whole-Poly factoring, ordering/representation of returned factors may differ (sorted by (exp, len(rep), len(poly.gens), rep) for method == 'sqf'), and this interaction was not verified by the investigation (risk to callers that depend on previous ordering).
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with SymPy imported: from sympy import symbols, sqf_list, expand
Call sqf_list on the product: sqf_list(prod)
Call sqf_list on the expanded polynomial: sqf_list(expand(prod))
Compare the two results (they should be the same set of factor/exponent pairs).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: sqf_list should return a canonical, syntax-independent square-free factorization for a polynomial expression. For the repro input, the factors should be grouped so that the quadratic factor formed by (x - 2)*(x - 3) appears as (x**2 - 5*x + 6) with multiplicity 3 (i.e., (1, [(x**2 + 1, 1), (x - 1, 2), (x**2 - 5*x + 6, 3)]) up to ordering).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When given a product of multiplicands, sqf_list returns separate linear factors for x - 2 and x - 3 each with multiplicity 3, e.g. (1, [(x**2 + 1, 1), (x - 1, 2), (x - 3, 3), (x - 2, 3)]), whereas when given the expanded/Poly form the factors are grouped into the quadratic (x**2 - 5*x + 6) with multiplicity 3.

hints_text:
I guess correct can be either the first or the second. But we should stick to it.

This [SO post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57536689/sympys-sqf-and-sqf-list-give-different-results-once-i-use-poly-or-as-pol) highlights another problem, too:

```python
>>> v = (x1 + 2) ** 2 * (x2 + 4) ** 5
>>> sqf(v)
(x1 + 2)**2*(x2 + 4)**5
>>> sqf(v.expand())
(x1 + 2)**2  <-- where is the x2 factor?
```
The documentation is incomplete. The docstrings for low level methods in `sqfreetools` show that they are for univariate polynomials only but that is missing from `polytools`. The docstrings should be amended.

The issue in OP is valid. The `Poly` method works as expected:
```
>>> Poly((x**2 + 1)*(x - 1)**2*(x - 2)**3*(x - 3)**3, x).sqf_list()
(1, [(Poly(x**2 + 1, x, domain='ZZ'), 1), (Poly(x - 1, x, domain='ZZ'), 2), (Poly(x**2 - 5*x + 6, x, domain='ZZ'), 3)])
```
The two factors of multiplicity 3 are combined as they should be.

The `sqf_list` function fails to do that.
```
>>> sqf_list((x**2 + 1)*(x - 1)**2*(x - 2)**3*(x - 3)**3, x)
(1, [(x**2 + 1, 1), (x - 1, 2), (x - 3, 3), (x - 2, 3)])
```
It should scan the generic factor list and combine factors of same multiplicity before returning the list.
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/e4259125f63727b76d0a0c4743ba1cd8d433d3ea/sympy/polys/polytools.py#L6218
Hi, I am new to the sympy community and was looking to contribute to the project. I wanted to ask @akritas what's wrong in having 2 factors of multiplicity 3? Also, if the second issue (on SO) is still open, then I would like to work on it, @jksuom can you guide me from where I should start? 


Sent from my iPad

> On 15 Dec 2019, at 5:24 PM, Akhil Rajput <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> ﻿
> Hi, I am new to the sympy community and was looking to contribute to the project. I wanted to ask @akritas what's wrong in having 2 factors of multiplicity 3?
> 
Hi, 

The square free algorithm should pull out all factors of _same_ degree and present them as one product of given multiplicity (in this case one factor with roots of multiplicity 3).
> Also, if the second issue (on SO) is still open, then I would like to work on it, @jksuom can you guide me from where I should start?
> 
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub, or unsubscribe.

I would start with the docstrings. The squarefree methods are intended for univariate polynomials. The generator should be given as an input parameter. It may be omitted if there is no danger of confusion (only one symbol in the expression). Otherwise the result may be indeterminate as shown by the [example above](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/8695#issuecomment-522278244).
@jksuom, I'm still unclear. There is already an option to pass generators as an argument to sqf_list(). Should the function automatically find the generators present in the expression? Please guide me what should I do. 
If there is only one symbol in the expression, then the function can find the generator automatically. Otherwise I think that exactly one symbol should be given as the generator.

Moreover, I would like to change the implementations of `sqf_list()` and related functions so that they would be based on the corresponding `Poly` methods. Then they would start by converting the input expression into `p = Poly(f, *gens, **args)` and check that `p` has exactly one generator. Then `p.sqf_list()` etc, would be called.
Then what will happen in case of multiple generators? Just confirming, generators here refer to symbols/variables.
> generators here refer to symbols/variables.

Yes.
> Then what will happen in case of multiple generators?

I think that ValueError could be raised. It seems that some kind of result is currently returned but there is no documentation, and I don't know of any reasonable use where the ordinary factorization would not suffice.
> If there is only one symbol in the expression, then the function can find the generator automatically. Otherwise I think that exactly one symbol should be given as the generator.
> 
> Moreover, I would like to change the implementations of `sqf_list()` and related functions so that they would be based on the corresponding `Poly` methods. Then they would start by converting the input expression into `p = Poly(f, *gens, **args)` and check that `p` has exactly one generator. Then `p.sqf_list()` etc, would be called.

@jksuom  In the helper function __symbolic_factor_list_ of sqf_list, the expression is already being converted to polynomial and then corresponding _sqf_list_ function is called. So, I should just ensure if the number of generators passed is one?
> I should just ensure if the number of generators passed is one?

If there is exactly one generator passed, then it is possible to call `_generic_factor_list` with the given arguments. However, it is necessary to post-process the result. In the example above, it returns

    (1, [(x**2 + 1, 1), (x - 1, 2), (x - 3, 3), (x - 2, 3)])

while `sqf_list` should return only one polynomial for each power. Therefore the two threefold factors `x - 3` and `x - 2` should be combined to give a single `(x**2 - 5*x + 6, 3)`.

It is probably quite common that no generators are given, in particular, when the expression looks like a univariate polynomial. This should be acceptable but more work is then necessary to find the number of generators. I think that it is best to convert the expression to a `Poly` object to see the generators. If there is only one, then the `sqf_list` method can be called, otherwise a `ValueError` should be raised.

It is possible that the latter procedure will be more efficient even if a single generator is given.
@jksuom I have created a PR (#18307)for the issue. I haven't done anything for multiple generator case as it was ambiguous. It would be great if you could review it. Thank you. 
@jksuom what can be done in case if the expression given is a constant (without any generators)? For example:  `sqf_list(1)`. We won't be able to construct a polynomial and PolificationFailed error will be raised.
I think that the error can be raised. It is typical of many polynomial functions that they don't work with constant expressions.

-    parameter ``result``  is used internally; it is not necessary to pass
-    anything for this.
+    parameter ``result``  is used internally; it is not necessary

+
+    Note: changing the sequence during iteration will raise a

@@ -2106,15 +2111,27 @@ def uniq(seq, result=None):

+    try:
+        n = len(seq)
+    except TypeError:
+        n = None
+    def check():
+        # check that size of seq did not change during iteration;
+        # if n == None the object won't support size changing, e.g.
+        # an iterator can't be changed
+        if n is not None and len(seq) != n:
+            raise RuntimeError('sequence changed size during iteration')
     try:
         seen = set()
         result = result or []
         for i, s in enumerate(seq):
             if not (s in seen or seen.add(s)):
                 yield s
+                check()
     except TypeError:
         if s not in result:
             yield s
+            check()
             result.append(s)
         if hasattr(seq, '__getitem__'):
             for s in uniq(seq[i + 1:], result):

problem_statement.Title: uniq silently yields wrong results when caller mutates input sequence during iteration
problem_statement.Description: When a caller mutates (resizes) a sequence passed to sympy.utilities.iterables.uniq while iterating its results, uniq does not detect the size change and yields a silently incorrect/partial result instead of raising an error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) uniq assumes the input sequence's size is stable during iteration. Location: sympy/utilities/iterables.py::uniq. Evidence: implementation uses `for i, s in enumerate(seq):` and there is no capture or comparison of len(seq).

2) The except TypeError (unhashable-element) control path also yields/recurses without any size check. Location: sympy/utilities/iterables.py::uniq (except TypeError branch around lines ~2115–2124). Evidence: that branch yields `s` and calls uniq(...) with no len check.

3) The correct relationship (diagnostic requirement encoded by the investigation) is: when the sequence supports length, record initial_len = len(seq) (agent variable name: initial_len) and set can_check_len = hasattr(seq, '__len__'). Before every yield (in the main loop and at the top of the except TypeError branch) do: if can_check_len and len(seq) != initial_len: raise RuntimeError("sequence changed size during iteration"). This string and those variable names/semantics were identified as the intended check.

4) The test-suite does not assert this behavior. Location: sympy/utilities/tests/test_iterables.py (existing uniq tests around lines ~695–703). Evidence: no test currently verifies that mutating a sequence during uniq iteration raises RuntimeError; a new test (e.g. test_uniq_raises_on_sequence_mutation) is required to lock in the behavior.

5) uniq is used in many call sites (examples: sympy/crypto/crypto.py, sympy/combinatorics/*, sympy/core/compatibility.py, sympy/geometry/plane.py). Location: search results for occurrences of `uniq(` across the codebase. Evidence: codebase search. Impact note (diagnostic): callers that intentionally mutate the input during uniq iteration (if any exist) would observe a new RuntimeError instead of previously silent incorrect output.

6) Investigation gaps that affect confidence in the diagnosis: the agent did not execute the code paths or tests, and did not exercise the except TypeError path with concrete inputs (e.g., unhashable element at position 0) to validate variable bindings and behavior. Location: agent analysis notes. Evidence: agent flagged these omissions. These are outstanding validation items, not contradictory findings.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python session with SymPy import path available.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If the caller mutates (changes the size of) the sequence passed to uniq during iteration, iteration should raise a RuntimeError (user-visible) indicating the sequence changed size during iteration; it should not silently return a wrong or partial set of values.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: uniq yields a subset of elements silently when the underlying list is mutated during iteration (example from repro: it prints only 'T', 'I', 'L') and no error is raised, producing incorrect results without notification.

hints_text:
I'm not sure there is a need to handle this case. Users should know not to mutate something while iterating over it.
With regards to the above discussion, I believe it would indeed be helpful if modifying a passed list to ``uniq`` raises an error while iterating over it, because it does not immediately follow that ``uniq(f)`` would get updated if ``f`` gets updated, as the user might think something like ``uniq`` stores a copy of ``f``, computes the list of unique elements in it, and returns that list. The user may not know, that yield is being used internally instead of return.

I have a doubt regarding the implementation of ``uniq``:
[https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/5bfe93281866f0841b36a429f4090c04a0e81d21/sympy/utilities/iterables.py#L2109-L2124](url)
Here, if the first argument, ``seq`` in ``uniq`` does not have a ``__getitem__`` method, and a TypeError is raised somehow, then we call the ``uniq`` function again on ``seq`` with the updated ``result``, won't that yield ALL of the elements of ``seq`` again, even those which have already been _yielded_? 
So mainly what I wanted to point out was, that if we're assuming that the given ``seq`` is iterable (which we must, since we pass it on to the ``enumerate`` function), by definition, ``seq`` must have either ``__getitem__`` or ``__iter__``, both of which can be used to iterate over the **remaining elements** if the TypeError is raised. 
Also, I'm unable to understand the role of ``result`` in all of this, kindly explain.

So should I work on the error handling bit in this function?

problem_statement.Title: BlockMatrix._entry descends into a sub-block for symbolic indices causing wrong element selection
problem_statement.Description: Indexing a BlockMatrix with a symbolic row index can be simplified incorrectly to an element of a specific sub-block. Example: for C = BlockMatrix([[A],[B]]) with A shape 1x1 and B shape n x 1, C[i,0] is simplified to A[i,0] even though the global index i may refer to a row in A or in B.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Over-eager block selection in BlockMatrix._entry (sympy/matrices/expressions/blockmatrix.py:BlockMatrix._entry). The method treats relational boundary tests (e.g. i < boundary) involving symbolic indices as if they determine membership; non-provable relations are used to pick a block, causing descent into that block and returning that block's element.

2) Inconsistent contract between BlockMatrix._entry and atomic MatrixSymbol._entry (sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py::MatrixSymbol._entry). MatrixSymbol._entry returns MatrixElement(self,i,j) (conservative behavior). BlockMatrix._entry does not mirror this conservative behavior when block membership is not provable, leading to premature rewriting to a child block element.

3) MatrixExpr.__getitem__ (sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py::MatrixExpr.__getitem__) delegates to self._entry(i,j). Because _entry can return a sub-block element, __getitem__ will emit that already-resolved expression; this is the call site where BlockMatrix's over-eager descent produces the visible wrong result.

4) MatrixElement.doit expands MatrixElement(parent,i,j) to parent[i,j] (sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py::MatrixElement.doit). When parent is a BlockMatrix, this triggers BlockMatrix._entry; therefore BlockMatrix._entry must be conservative in ambiguous cases so MatrixElement does not expand into a wrong sub-block element.

5) Incorrect handling of local-index mapping and provability criteria. When descent is actually safe, the local-row index must be computed exactly as:
   local_i = i - sum(previous_row_block_sizes)
   (and similarly for columns). The current logic descends without requiring that a boundary relational test is provably True. The correct decision criterion (as identified by the investigation) is: only descend into a candidate block if the membership relation is provably True (e.g. using a provability test such as ask(Q.is_true(i < boundary)) or testing (i < boundary) == True). Treating a non-False relational expression as sufficient to pick a block is the wrong assumption.

6) Code-level evidence: blockmatrix.py imports ask and Q (provability utilities) but the existing tests use relational results as truthy rather than requiring provable True; this encodes the wrong assumption in BlockMatrix._entry and is the immediate cause of the observed simplification C[i,0] -> A[i,0].
StepsToReproduce: Run Python with SymPy installed and execute the following:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When indexed with a symbolic global index that is not provably in one block, C[i,0] should remain an unresolved reference to the BlockMatrix element (e.g. display as C[i,0] or MatrixElement(C, i, 0)), not be simplified to an element of a specific sub-block.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For the given example the output shows the BlockMatrix C but prints C[i,0] as (A)[i,0] (i.e. the expression is simplified to the element of sub-block A even though i may refer to rows in A or B).

I also think that `C[3, 0]` should be undefined because there is no guarantee that n is sufficiently large to contain elements.
`C[3, 0]` should just stay unevaluated, since it might be valid (I assume that's what you mean by 'undefined'). It should be possible to handle some cases properly, for example `C[n, 0]` should return `B[n - 1, 0]`.

If I get some time I might have a go at it, seems to be a nice first PR.

**EDIT:** Sorry that's not even true. If `n` is zero, then `C[n, 0]` is not `B[n - 1, 0]`.

diff --git a/sympy/polys/factortools.py b/sympy/polys/factortools.py
--- a/sympy/polys/factortools.py
+++ b/sympy/polys/factortools.py
@@ -124,13 +124,64 @@ def dmp_trial_division(f, factors, u, K):
 
 
 def dup_zz_mignotte_bound(f, K):
-    """Mignotte bound for univariate polynomials in `K[x]`. """
-    a = dup_max_norm(f, K)
-    b = abs(dup_LC(f, K))
-    n = dup_degree(f)
+    """
+    The Knuth-Cohen variant of Mignotte bound for
+    univariate polynomials in `K[x]`.
 
-    return K.sqrt(K(n + 1))*2**n*a*b
+    Examples
+    ========
+
+    >>> from sympy.polys import ring, ZZ
+    >>> R, x = ring("x", ZZ)
+
+    >>> f = x**3 + 14*x**2 + 56*x + 64
+    >>> R.dup_zz_mignotte_bound(f)
+    152
+
+    By checking `factor(f)` we can see that max coeff is 8
+
+    Also consider a case that `f` is irreducible for example `f = 2*x**2 + 3*x + 4`
+    To avoid a bug for these cases, we return the bound plus the max coefficient of `f`
+
+    >>> f = 2*x**2 + 3*x + 4
+    >>> R.dup_zz_mignotte_bound(f)
+    6
+
+    Lastly,To see the difference between the new and the old Mignotte bound
+    consider the irreducible polynomial::
+
+    >>> f = 87*x**7 + 4*x**6 + 80*x**5 + 17*x**4 + 9*x**3 + 12*x**2 + 49*x + 26
+    >>> R.dup_zz_mignotte_bound(f)
+    744
+
+    The new Mignotte bound is 744 whereas the old one (SymPy 1.5.1) is 1937664.
+
+
+    References
+    ==========
+
+    ..[1] [Abbott2013]_
+
+    """
+    from sympy import binomial
+
+    d = dup_degree(f)
+    delta = _ceil(d / 2)
+    delta2 = _ceil(delta / 2)
+
+    # euclidean-norm
+    eucl_norm = K.sqrt( sum( [cf**2 for cf in f] ) )
+
+    # biggest values of binomial coefficients (p. 538 of reference)
+    t1 = binomial(delta - 1, delta2)
+    t2 = binomial(delta - 1, delta2 - 1)
+
+    lc = K.abs(dup_LC(f, K))   # leading coefficient
+    bound = t1 * eucl_norm + t2 * lc   # (p. 538 of reference)
+    bound += dup_max_norm(f, K) # add max coeff for irreducible polys
+    bound = _ceil(bound / 2) * 2   # round up to even integer
 
+    return bound
 
 def dmp_zz_mignotte_bound(f, u, K):
     """Mignotte bound for multivariate polynomials in `K[X]`. """

problem_statement.Title: sympy.polys.factortools uses Mignotte bound (duplicated inline) instead of Knuth–Cohen; Knuth–Cohen not implemented or validated
problem_statement.Description: The coefficient-bound functions in sympy/polys/factortools.py compute and use the Mignotte expression B = sqrt(n+1)*2^n*a*b (univariate and multivariate variants). This assumption is encoded in multiple places (including an inline copy in dup_zz_zassenhaus) and the proposed tighter Knuth–Cohen bound has not been implemented or validated as a replacement.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/polys/factortools.py::dup_zz_mignotte_bound encodes the canonical bound as the Mignotte expression B = K.sqrt(K(n + 1))*2**n*a*b (where a = dup_max_norm(f,K), b = abs(dup_LC(f,K)), n = dup_degree(f)). This is the primary incorrect assumption (function and location named).

2) sympy/polys/factortools.py::dmp_zz_mignotte_bound applies the identical Mignotte formula to multivariate polynomials using n = sum(dmp_degree_list(f,u)), i.e. it assumes summing degrees generalizes the univariate exponent factor; that aggregation is an encoded incorrect assumption (function and location named).

3) sympy/polys/factortools.py::dup_zz_zassenhaus duplicates the Mignotte expression inline as B = int(abs(K.sqrt(K(n + 1))*2**n*A*b)) instead of calling the central bound API; this duplication encodes the same wrong assumption in a second location and risks divergence if only the API is changed.

4) sympy/polys/factortools.py::dmp_zz_wang and other callers select primes with p = nextprime(bound) where bound is dmp_zz_mignotte_bound(f,u,K); those call-sites assume the Mignotte bound provides the safety margin required for modular prime selection and Hensel-lifting—this usage is an encoded assumption that must be validated when changing the bound.

5) sympy/polys/tests/test_factortools.py contains unit tests that hardcode numeric outputs derived from the Mignotte bound (examples: assert R.dup_zz_mignotte_bound(2*x**2 + 3*x + 4) == 32; p = ZZ(nextprime(R.dmp_zz_mignotte_bound(w_1))); assert p == 6291469). Those tests encode the expectation of the Mignotte-based numeric values and will fail if the bound changes.

6) The proposed replacement, the Knuth–Cohen bound, has not been implemented or numerically verified: there is no dup_zz_knuth_cohen_bound/dmp_zz_knuth_cohen_bound code and no recomputed test values or validation that the (smaller/tighter) Knuth–Cohen bound still provides the guarantees required for prime selection/Hensel procedures. This missing implementation/verification is an explicit gap (location: investigation/patch area).
StepsToReproduce: In a Python environment with sympy available, from sympy import symbols; from sympy.polys import factortools as R; x = symbols('x')
Call R.dup_zz_mignotte_bound(2*x**2 + 3*x + 4) and observe the returned integer (current behavior returns 32).
Call R.dmp_zz_mignotte_bound(<multivariate polynomial w_1>, <vars>, ZZ) and observe returned bound; compute p = ZZ(nextprime(bound)) and observe p (current test expects 6291469 for the provided w_1).
Run the test file sympy/polys/tests/test_factortools.py and observe assertions that expect Mignotte-based numeric values.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
The public bound APIs (dup_zz_mignotte_bound and dmp_zz_mignotte_bound) should return a valid coefficient bound appropriate for use by modular prime selection and Hensel-lifting. In user-visible terms this means:
- The bound values returned should be correct (i.e., provably safe for downstream modular algorithms) and, where possible, tighter than the current Mignotte expressions (the intended Knuth–Cohen bound outcome).
- Callers that select primes based on the bound should receive primes that are safely larger than coefficient bounds and modular/Hensel-based factorization should succeed.
- Unit tests should assert numeric values consistent with the chosen valid bound.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Currently the code returns and uses the Mignotte-based bound B = K.sqrt(K(n + 1))*2**n*a*b in dup_zz_mignotte_bound and dmp_zz_mignotte_bound (with n = sum(dmp_degree_list(...)) for multivariate). dup_zz_zassenhaus contains an inline copy of the same expression. Tests and some code paths expect exact numeric values derived from that Mignotte expression (e.g., 32 and prime 6291469). The Knuth–Cohen bound has not been implemented or validated in the codebase, and numeric test values for it have not been computed.

problem_statement.Title: sign.rewrite(Abs) is a no-op (missing _eval_rewrite_as_Abs) and zero-case semantics are inconsistent
problem_statement.Description: Calling sign(x).rewrite(Abs) does not produce the algebraic form x/Abs(x); rewrite leaves sign unchanged. Additionally, sign(0) is defined as 0 while the algebraic form z/Abs(z) (and Abs->sign rewrite) yields NaN at zero, creating a semantic mismatch.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing rewrite helper on sign: class sign in sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py does not implement _eval_rewrite_as_Abs. Basic.rewrite looks for a method named '_eval_rewrite_as_Abs' and will not rewrite sign(x) to an Abs-based expression without it. The algebraic identity that should be exposed is sign(z) -> z/Abs(z). The required helper signature (as used by Basic.rewrite) is: def _eval_rewrite_as_Abs(self, arg, **kwargs) and it should return arg/Abs(arg).

2) Asymmetric existing behavior: Abs already implements the inverse helper Abs._eval_rewrite_as_sign returning arg/sign(arg) (sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py:Abs). The absence of the symmetric sign->_eval_rewrite_as_Abs is an omission (not a design restriction) and is why rewrite fails to produce the expected expression.

3) Rewrite dispatch contract: sympy/core/basic.py:rewrite and _eval_rewrite construct the rule name '_eval_rewrite_as_' + TargetClassName and call that method with signature (self, arg, **kwargs). This exact naming/signature requirement is why the missing method on sign prevents the rewrite.

4) Zero-case semantic mismatch (inconsistent definitions): sign.eval defines sign(0) == 0 (sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py:sign.eval). However, the algebraic expression z/Abs(z) (and Abs._eval_rewrite_as_sign -> arg/sign(arg)) yields an undefined/NaN form at z == 0 (0/Abs(0) or 0/0). Thus, if sign is rewritten to arg/Abs(arg), the evaluated result for the literal 0 will differ (NaN) from sign(0) (0). This inconsistency is encoded in sign.eval and in Abs._eval_rewrite_as_sign and remains an unresolved semantic decision.

5) No numeric constant change: there are no incorrect numeric constants; the defect is a missing helper method and a semantic inconsistency around zero. Note: the agent investigation did not enumerate repository call-sites/tests that may rely on the previous absence of sign.rewrite(Abs); adding the helper could affect tests/usages that assumed no rewrite.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import symbols, sign, Abs
Evaluate sign(x).rewrite(Abs) and observe it does not produce x/Abs(x).
Evaluate sign(0) and observe it returns 0.
Evaluate 0/Abs(0) and observe it evaluates to nan.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When rewriting sign to an Abs-based expression, sign(z).rewrite(Abs) should produce the algebraic form z/Abs(z) for complex non-zero z (i.e., expose the identity sign(z) = z/Abs(z) for nonzero arguments).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: sign(x).rewrite(Abs) is a no-op (rewrite leaves sign(x) unchanged). sign(0) evaluates to 0, while the algebraic forms z/Abs(z) or arg/sign(arg) evaluate to NaN at z==0, producing inconsistent results between direct evaluation and rewritten expressions.

hints_text:
Getting nan for `sign(0)` would be pretty [non-intuitive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sign_function) for any mathematical programmer given it's non-derivative definition.

If a rewrite request cannot be fulfilled under all conditions and the request was not for Piecewise, I think the rewrite should return None.
Actually I think it's fine if the rewrite doesn't always work. At least something like this could rewrite:
```julia
In [2]: sign(1+I).rewrite(Abs)                                                                                                                 
Out[2]: sign(1 + ⅈ)
```
You can use piecewise like
```
Piecewise(
    (0, Eq(x, 0)),
    (x / Abs(x), Ne(x, 0))
)
```
Originally this question comes from SO:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61676438/integrating-and-deriving-absolute-functions-sympy/61681347#61681347

The original question was about `diff(Abs(x))`:
```
In [2]: x = Symbol('x', real=True)                                                                                                             

In [3]: Abs(x).diff(x)                                                                                                                         
Out[3]: sign(x)
```
Maybe the result from `diff` should be a `Piecewise` or at least an `ExprCondPair` guarding against `x=0`.
The problem is that real-valued functions like abs, re, im, arg,... are not holomorphic and have no complex derivative. See also https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/8502.
@jksuom could we add conditions in the `Derivative` class of the functions module which would check if the expression is an instance of a non-holomorphic function, in such a case it could raise an error or in the case of `Abs` simply  check the domain. I believe all the classes in `sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py` could be checked.
Would it be possible to add an `_eval_derivative` method raising an error to those functions?
When would it raise?
If the function is non-holomorphic, there is no derivative to be returned.
There is a reasonable derivative of `Abs` when defined over the reals though e.g.:
```julia
In [1]: x = Symbol('x', real=True)                                                                                                

In [2]: Abs(x).diff(x)                                                                                                            
Out[2]: sign(x)
```
Maybe there should be two functions, one defined on reals and the other on complexes.
> Would it be possible to add an `_eval_derivative` method raising an error to those functions?

In the `Derivative` class in `sympy.function`?



> When would it raise?

As suggested, if the function is non-holomorphic or in the case of `Abs()` it could be a check on the domain of the argument.


> Maybe there should be two functions, one defined on reals and the other on complexes.

I am not sure if there are any non-holomorphic functions on Real numbers. In my opinion only the `Abs()` function would fall in this case. Hence I think this could be done using one function only.
```
def _eval_derivative(self, expr):
    if isinstance(expr,[re, im, sign, arg, conjugate]):
	raise TypeError("Derivative not possible for Non-Holomorphic functions")
    if isinstance(expr,Abs):
	if Abs.arg[0].free_symbols.is_complex:
	    raises TypeError("There is a complex argument which makes Abs non-holomorphic")
```
This is something I was thinking but I am not sure about it as `Derivative` class already has a method with the same name. I also think that appropriate changes also need to be made in the `fdiff()` method of the `Abs` class.
@jksuom I wanted to know if there are more non-holomorphic functions in sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py to which an error can be raised.
Those functions in complexes.py have a `_eval_derivative` method. Maybe that would be the proper place for raising an error if that is desired.
Are there any other examples of functions that raise when differentiated?

I just tried
```julia
In [83]: n = Symbol('n', integer=True, positive=True)                                                                             

In [84]: totient(n).diff(n)                                                                                                       
Out[84]: 
d             
──(totient(n))
dn 
```
@oscarbenjamin I am not sure if this is a situation when it should raise, for example: if `n` here is a prime number the derivative wrt `n` would hence be `1` . Although in sympy 
```
>>> x = Symbol('x', real=True, prime=True)
>>> totient(x).evalf()
ϕ(x)
```
is the output and not `x-1`.Maybe this kind of functionality can be added.
@jksuom I think your way is correct and wanted to ask if the error to be raised is appropriately `TypeError`?
I don't think that the totient function should be differentiable. I was just trying to think of functions where it might be an error to differentiate them.

I think it's better to leave the derivative of Abs unevaluated. You might have something like `Abs(f(x))` where `f` can be substituted for something reasonable later.
@dhruvmendiratta6 Yes, I think that `TypeError` would be the appropriate choice. Note, however, that raising errors would probably break some tests. It may be desirable to add some try-except blocks to handle those properly.
What about something like this:
```julia
In [21]: x = Symbol('x', real=True)                                                                                               

In [22]: f = Function('f')                                                                                                        

In [23]: e = Derivative(Abs(f(x)), x)                                                                                             

In [24]: e                                                                                                                        
Out[24]: 
d         
──(│f(x)│)
dx        

In [25]: e.subs(f, cosh)                                                                                                          
Out[25]: 
d          
──(cosh(x))
dx         

In [26]: e.subs(f, cosh).doit()                                                                                                   
Out[26]: sinh(x)
```
@jksuom @oscarbenjamin 
Any suggestion on how this can be done?
I think changes need to be made here
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/7c11a00d4ace555e8be084d69c4da4e6f4975f64/sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py#L605-L608
to leave the derivative of `Abs` unevaluated. I tried changing this to 
```
def _eval_derivative(self, x):
        if self.args[0].is_extended_real or self.args[0].is_imaginary:
            return Derivative(self.args[0], x, evaluate=True) \
                * Derivative(self, x, evaluate=False)
```
which gives
```
>>> x = Symbol('x', real = True)
>>> Abs(x**3).diff(x)
x**2*Derivative(Abs(x), x) + 2*x*Abs(x)
```
But then I can't figure out how to evaluate when the need arises.The above result,which I think is wrong, occurs even when no changes are made.
I think rewrite in general can't avoid having situations where things are only defined correctly in the limit, unless we return a Piecewise. For example, `sinc(x).rewrite(sin)`.
```py
>>> pprint(sinc(x).rewrite(sin))
⎧sin(x)
⎪──────  for x ≠ 0
⎨  x
⎪
⎩  1     otherwise
```
I made `_eval_rewrite_as_Abs()` for the `sign` class which gives the following:
```
>>> sign(x).rewrite(Abs)
Piecewise((0, Eq(x, 0)), (x/Abs(x), True))
```
Although as discussed earlier raising an error in `_eval_derivative()` causes some tests to break :
```
File "c:\users\mendiratta\sympy\sympy\functions\elementary\tests\test_complexes.py", line 414, in test_Abs
    assert Abs(x).diff(x) == -sign(x)
 File "c:\users\mendiratta\sympy\sympy\functions\elementary\tests\test_complexes.py", line 833, in test_derivatives_issue_4757
    assert Abs(f(x)).diff(x).subs(f(x), 1 + I*x).doit() == x/sqrt(1 + x**2)
 File "c:\users\mendiratta\sympy\sympy\functions\elementary\tests\test_complexes.py", line 969, in test_issue_15893
    assert eq.doit() == sign(f(x))
```
The first two are understood but in the third one both `f` and `x` are real and still are caught by the newly raised error which doesn't make sense as I raised a `TypeError` only if the argument is not real.

diff --git a/sympy/physics/vector/point.py b/sympy/physics/vector/point.py
--- a/sympy/physics/vector/point.py
+++ b/sympy/physics/vector/point.py
@@ -483,19 +483,49 @@ def vel(self, frame):
         Examples
         ========
 
-        >>> from sympy.physics.vector import Point, ReferenceFrame
+        >>> from sympy.physics.vector import Point, ReferenceFrame, dynamicsymbols
         >>> N = ReferenceFrame('N')
         >>> p1 = Point('p1')
         >>> p1.set_vel(N, 10 * N.x)
         >>> p1.vel(N)
         10*N.x
 
+        Velocities will be automatically calculated if possible, otherwise a ``ValueError`` will be returned. If it is possible to calculate multiple different velocities from the relative points, the points defined most directly relative to this point will be used. In the case of inconsistent relative positions of points, incorrect velocities may be returned. It is up to the user to define prior relative positions and velocities of points in a self-consistent way.
+
+        >>> p = Point('p')
+        >>> q = dynamicsymbols('q')
+        >>> p.set_vel(N, 10 * N.x)
+        >>> p2 = Point('p2')
+        >>> p2.set_pos(p, q*N.x)
+        >>> p2.vel(N)
+        (Derivative(q(t), t) + 10)*N.x
+
         """
 
         _check_frame(frame)
         if not (frame in self._vel_dict):
-            raise ValueError('Velocity of point ' + self.name + ' has not been'
+            visited = []
+            queue = [self]
+            while queue: #BFS to find nearest point
+                node = queue.pop(0)
+                if node not in visited:
+                    visited.append(node)
+                    for neighbor, neighbor_pos in node._pos_dict.items():
+                        try:
+                            neighbor_pos.express(frame) #Checks if pos vector is valid
+                        except ValueError:
+                            continue
+                        try :
+                            neighbor_velocity = neighbor._vel_dict[frame] #Checks if point has its vel defined in req frame
+                        except KeyError:
+                            queue.append(neighbor)
+                            continue
+                        self.set_vel(frame, self.pos_from(neighbor).dt(frame) + neighbor_velocity)
+                        return self._vel_dict[frame]
+            else:
+                raise ValueError('Velocity of point ' + self.name + ' has not been'
                              ' defined in ReferenceFrame ' + frame.name)
+
         return self._vel_dict[frame]
 
     def partial_velocity(self, frame, *gen_speeds):

diff --git a/sympy/physics/vector/tests/test_point.py b/sympy/physics/vector/tests/test_point.py
--- a/sympy/physics/vector/tests/test_point.py
+++ b/sympy/physics/vector/tests/test_point.py
@@ -126,3 +126,107 @@ def test_point_partial_velocity():
     assert p.partial_velocity(N, u1) == A.x
     assert p.partial_velocity(N, u1, u2) == (A.x, N.y)
     raises(ValueError, lambda: p.partial_velocity(A, u1))
+
+def test_point_vel(): #Basic functionality
+    q1, q2 = dynamicsymbols('q1 q2')
+    N = ReferenceFrame('N')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    Q = Point('Q')
+    O = Point('O')
+    Q.set_pos(O, q1 * N.x)
+    raises(ValueError , lambda: Q.vel(N)) # Velocity of O in N is not defined
+    O.set_vel(N, q2 * N.y)
+    assert O.vel(N) == q2 * N.y
+    raises(ValueError , lambda : O.vel(B)) #Velocity of O is not defined in B
+
+def test_auto_point_vel():
+    t = dynamicsymbols._t
+    q1, q2 = dynamicsymbols('q1 q2')
+    N = ReferenceFrame('N')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    O = Point('O')
+    Q = Point('Q')
+    Q.set_pos(O, q1 * N.x)
+    O.set_vel(N, q2 * N.y)
+    assert Q.vel(N) == q1.diff(t) * N.x + q2 * N.y  # Velocity of Q using O
+    P1 = Point('P1')
+    P1.set_pos(O, q1 * B.x)
+    P2 = Point('P2')
+    P2.set_pos(P1, q2 * B.z)
+    raises(ValueError, lambda : P2.vel(B)) # O's velocity is defined in different frame, and no
+    #point in between has its velocity defined
+    raises(ValueError, lambda: P2.vel(N)) # Velocity of O not defined in N
+
+def test_auto_point_vel_multiple_point_path():
+    t = dynamicsymbols._t
+    q1, q2 = dynamicsymbols('q1 q2')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    P = Point('P')
+    P.set_vel(B, q1 * B.x)
+    P1 = Point('P1')
+    P1.set_pos(P, q2 * B.y)
+    P1.set_vel(B, q1 * B.z)
+    P2 = Point('P2')
+    P2.set_pos(P1, q1 * B.z)
+    P3 = Point('P3')
+    P3.set_pos(P2, 10 * q1 * B.y)
+    assert P3.vel(B) == 10 * q1.diff(t) * B.y + (q1 + q1.diff(t)) * B.z
+
+def test_auto_vel_dont_overwrite():
+    t = dynamicsymbols._t
+    q1, q2, u1 = dynamicsymbols('q1, q2, u1')
+    N = ReferenceFrame('N')
+    P = Point('P1')
+    P.set_vel(N, u1 * N.x)
+    P1 = Point('P1')
+    P1.set_pos(P, q2 * N.y)
+    assert P1.vel(N) == q2.diff(t) * N.y + u1 * N.x
+    assert P.vel(N) == u1 * N.x
+    P1.set_vel(N, u1 * N.z)
+    assert P1.vel(N) == u1 * N.z
+
+def test_auto_point_vel_if_tree_has_vel_but_inappropriate_pos_vector():
+    q1, q2 = dynamicsymbols('q1 q2')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    S = ReferenceFrame('S')
+    P = Point('P')
+    P.set_vel(B, q1 * B.x)
+    P1 = Point('P1')
+    P1.set_pos(P, S.y)
+    raises(ValueError, lambda : P1.vel(B)) # P1.pos_from(P) can't be expressed in B
+    raises(ValueError, lambda : P1.vel(S)) # P.vel(S) not defined
+
+def test_auto_point_vel_shortest_path():
+    t = dynamicsymbols._t
+    q1, q2, u1, u2 = dynamicsymbols('q1 q2 u1 u2')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    P = Point('P')
+    P.set_vel(B, u1 * B.x)
+    P1 = Point('P1')
+    P1.set_pos(P, q2 * B.y)
+    P1.set_vel(B, q1 * B.z)
+    P2 = Point('P2')
+    P2.set_pos(P1, q1 * B.z)
+    P3 = Point('P3')
+    P3.set_pos(P2, 10 * q1 * B.y)
+    P4 = Point('P4')
+    P4.set_pos(P3, q1 * B.x)
+    O = Point('O')
+    O.set_vel(B, u2 * B.y)
+    O1 = Point('O1')
+    O1.set_pos(O, q2 * B.z)
+    P4.set_pos(O1, q1 * B.x + q2 * B.z)
+    assert P4.vel(B) == q1.diff(t) * B.x + u2 * B.y + 2 * q2.diff(t) * B.z
+
+def test_auto_point_vel_connected_frames():
+    t = dynamicsymbols._t
+    q, q1, q2, u = dynamicsymbols('q q1 q2 u')
+    N = ReferenceFrame('N')
+    B = ReferenceFrame('B')
+    O = Point('O')
+    O.set_vel(N, u * N.x)
+    P = Point('P')
+    P.set_pos(O, q1 * N.x + q2 * B.y)
+    raises(ValueError, lambda: P.vel(N))
+    N.orient(B, 'Axis', (q, B.x))
+    assert P.vel(N) == (u + q1.diff(t)) * N.x + q2.diff(t) * B.y - q2 * q.diff(t) * B.z

problem_statement.Title: Point.vel raises ValueError instead of deriving velocity from position (Point.vel / Point.acc incorrect assumptions)
problem_statement.Description: Calling Point.vel(frame) raises ValueError when the requested frame key is not present in the point's internal velocity dictionary, even when the point's position relative to another point is known and its time derivative (including frame rotation terms) is computable.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/physics/vector/point.py:Point.vel — Incorrect assumption: vel() requires an explicit entry in self._vel_dict for the requested ReferenceFrame and immediately raises ValueError otherwise. Location: sympy/physics/vector/point.py::Point.vel. Evidence: current code raises 'Velocity of point <name> has not been defined in ReferenceFrame <frame>' when frame not in self._vel_dict, which is why Q.vel(A) raises. Correct relationship that is not used: when a point's position relative to another point is known, the point's velocity in a frame should be derivable via
   v_self(frame) = v_other(frame) + (pos_from(other)).dt(frame)
and if no connected point has an explicit velocity then the local derivative should at least be used as
   v_self(frame) = (pos_from(neighbor)).dt(frame)
The Vector.dt(pos, frame) already produces the correct time derivative including angular-velocity cross terms (component_frame.ang_vel_in(frame) ^ component_vector), so pos.dt(frame) yields the expected result (e.g. q'*A.x + 2*q'*A.y).

2) sympy/physics/vector/point.py:Point.acc — Incorrect assumption: acc() assumes self._vel_dict[frame] exists when acceleration is not explicitly stored and indexes it without membership checking, which can raise KeyError. Location: sympy/physics/vector/point.py::Point.acc. Evidence: code path that checks acc_dict membership then references self._vel_dict[frame] without checking frame presence. Correct relationship that is expected but not enforced: if acceleration is not stored then, if a velocity for the frame exists, acceleration should be the time derivative of that velocity; otherwise acceleration should be treated as zero (not raise KeyError).
StepsToReproduce: import sympy as sm; import sympy.physics.mechanics as me
Q.vel(A)                  # raises ValueError: Velocity of point Q has not been defined in ReferenceFrame A
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Point.vel(frame) should return the point's velocity expressed in the requested ReferenceFrame when that velocity can be derived from known positional relationships and frame rotations. In the provided example, Q.vel(A) should return the time derivative of r in frame A (q'*A.x + 2*q'*A.y). More generally, if pos_from(other) and/or an explicit velocity for a connected point exist, the returned velocity should reflect v_self(frame) = v_other(frame) + (pos_from(other)).dt(frame) and include any angular-velocity cross terms computed by Vector.dt.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Point.vel(frame) raises ValueError('Velocity of point <name> has not been defined in ReferenceFrame <frame>') when the frame key is absent from the point's internal _vel_dict, even though the position was set and its time derivative is computable. Point.acc may raise KeyError by indexing _vel_dict[frame] without checking membership when acceleration is not explicitly stored.

problem_statement.Title: partitions() reuses the same mutable dict object on every yield, causing aliasing
problem_statement.Description: The partitions() iterator in sympy.utilities.iterables yields the same mutable dictionary object (ms) on every iteration and mutates it in-place between yields. Collecting its output (e.g. list(partitions(...))) produces many references to the same dict instead of independent partition dicts, which is surprising and bug-prone.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded in sympy/utilities/iterables.py::partitions: the function constructs a single dict named ms and yields that exact object on every iteration (yields `ms` or `(sum(ms.values()), ms)` when size=True). This encodes the assumption that callers will copy ms if they need independent dictionaries; as a result the same ms object is mutated and returned repeatedly, so collected results are multiple references to the final partition state.

2) Docstring in sympy/utilities/iterables.py explicitly states/assumes the reuse (contains the sentence that the same dictionary object is returned each time), reinforcing the incorrect expectation that callers must copy to get independent results. The docstring example/doctest is misleading under normal user expectations.

3) Related caller behavior (evidence of impact and compatibility): callers inside the repo (sympy/utilities/tests/test_iterables.py, sympy/utilities/iterables.py::multiset_partitions, sympy/combinatorics/partitions.py::IntegerPartition) either defensively call p.copy() or consume the dict immediately. This shows callers do not depend on ms identity, but also documents why users observed the surprising behavior in tests/examples.

4) Enumerative internals in sympy/utilities/enumerative.py (multiset_partitions_taocp and MultisetPartitionTraverser) intentionally reuse internal mutable state and yield internal lists/structures that are mutated in-place; those locations are a separate, intentional pattern and are not the bug. They are located in sympy/utilities/enumerative.py and must remain unchanged; partitions delegates to list_visitor for multiset cases which already returns fresh lists.

5) Exact behavior/formula evidence from code: partitions yields `ms` (the partition multiset dict) and when size=True yields `(sum(ms.values()), ms)`. Because ms is mutated after the yield, sum(ms.values()) may reflect the correct numeric size at yield time but collecting the yielded dict references later shows the final mutated ms unless the dict is copied at yield time.

6) Investigation gaps (scope/unknowns): the presented analysis did not run the full test suite or benchmarks to measure the performance/memory impact of returning independent dicts per yield, nor did it search for external/third-party users that might rely on the object-identity reuse. These are outstanding questions but do not alter the identified incorrect assumption encoded in partitions().
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python session with SymPy available.
Evaluate list(partitions(6, k=2)) and inspect the returned list elements (or print their id() values).
Observe that multiple list elements are the same object (mutually identical and equal to the final partition state).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Each yielded partition is an independent dictionary (or, when size=True, a tuple whose second element is an independent dictionary) representing the partition at the moment it was yielded. Collecting the generator (for example with list(partitions(...))) produces a list of distinct dict objects with the expected partition contents for each element.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: partitions() yields the same dict object ms on every iteration and mutates it in-place. As a result, collecting outputs (e.g. list(partitions(...))) produces a list of multiple references to the same dict, all reflecting the final mutated state. The docstring currently documents and demonstrates this reuse, which leads to surprising and error-prone behavior for callers that do not copy the yielded dicts.

problem_statement.Title: 0**-oo evaluates to 0 instead of ComplexInfinity (zoo)
problem_statement.Description: Evaluating 0**(-oo) in SymPy 1.5.1 returns 0, but the Pow documentation and expected mathematical semantics indicate it should return ComplexInfinity (zoo). The incorrect result arises during base-specific power reduction.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/numbers.py:Zero._eval_power assumes expt.is_negative covers negative infinity. It tests `expt.is_negative` to decide to return S.ComplexInfinity; however S.NegativeInfinity has is_negative == False and is_extended_negative == True, so -oo is missed and 0**-oo is not reduced to S.ComplexInfinity. (Location: sympy/core/numbers.py:Zero._eval_power)

2) sympy/core/numbers.py:Float._eval_power uses the same `expt.is_negative` check for base == 0 and currently returns S.Infinity for negative exponents. That check also misses expt == S.NegativeInfinity (is_negative == False), so Float(0)**(-oo) is not distinguished as an infinite extended-negative exponent. The correct relationship for Float(0) (per doc semantics encoded in other places) is: if expt.is_extended_negative and expt.is_infinite -> S.ComplexInfinity; if finite negative -> S.Infinity. (Location: sympy/core/numbers.py:Float._eval_power)

3) sympy/core/numbers.py:NegativeInfinity encodes extended negativity via is_extended_negative = True but does not set is_negative = True. Expr._eval_is_negative returns False for non-finite values; callers that assume expt.is_negative is True for -oo are relying on an incorrect assumption about the NegativeInfinity design. (Location: sympy/core/numbers.py:NegativeInfinity)

4) sympy/core/power.py:Pow.__new__ delegates to base._eval_power (calls b._eval_power(e)). This confirms the incorrect checks in Zero._eval_power/Float._eval_power are the right places where the wrong assumptions manifest as the observed incorrect evaluation. (Location: sympy/core/power.py:Pow.__new__)
StepsToReproduce: Install SymPy 1.5.1 and open a Python REPL with SymPy imported.
In the REPL run: from sympy import oo
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Per Pow documentation and mathematical convention for zero to a negative infinite exponent, 0**(-oo) should evaluate to ComplexInfinity (zoo). Similarly, a floating zero base (0.0)**(-oo) should be treated as an extended-negative infinite exponent and yield ComplexInfinity (zoo) rather than a finite numeric value.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In SymPy 1.5.1, evaluating 0**(-oo) returns 0 (an integer zero) instead of ComplexInfinity (zoo).

problem_statement.Title: expand_mul distributes multiplication into rounding functions (ceiling/floor/frac), causing inconsistent simplify() results
problem_statement.Description: When expansion with mul=True descends into function arguments, multiplication is distributed inside non-linear rounding functions (ceiling/floor/frac). This allows unsafe rewrites and produces different simplify() results depending on parsing/evaluation path (evaluate flag).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/function.py::expand_mul calls sympify(expr).expand(..., mul=True), which enables traversal into Function nodes and delegates to subclass _eval_expand_mul when present. (Location: sympy/core/function.py::expand_mul)

2) sympy/core/mul.py::Mul._eval_expand_mul unconditionally distributes multiplication over Adds (i.e., it will insert factors into Add nodes found inside function arguments) when mul=True. This distribution does not guard against non-linear functions such as rounding functions. (Location: sympy/core/mul.py::_eval_expand_mul)

3) Rounding functions (base class RoundFunction used by floor and ceiling) and frac do not implement an _eval_expand_mul hook to prevent unsafe distribution. Because they lack this guard, the global expansion machinery allows Mul._eval_expand_mul to change their internal Add structure. (Location: sympy/functions/elementary/integers.py::RoundFunction (floor, ceiling) and ::frac)

4) The combination of (1)-(3) encodes the incorrect assumption that it is always safe to distribute multiplication into every Function's argument. That assumption is incorrect for non-linear rounding/fractional-part functions and is the direct cause of the observed inconsistent simplify() outputs. (Related locations: sympy/core/expr.py::_expand_hint dispatcher, sympy/core/function.py::expand_mul, sympy/core/mul.py::_eval_expand_mul, sympy/functions/elementary/integers.py)

Note: The investigation identified the precise guard that is missing: RoundFunction and frac should provide an _eval_expand_mul implementation that prevents Mul._eval_expand_mul from distributing into their argument (i.e., arrange for inner expansion with mul disabled). This pinpoints where the incorrect assumption is encoded; no numeric constants or thresholds are involved.
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with sympy (example shown for the versions where behavior differs).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling simplify() on the same expression should produce the same simplified, user-visible result regardless of whether sympify(..., evaluate=True) or sympify(..., evaluate=False) was used to parse the expression. For the reported example, both parse paths should yield the same ceiling-containing expression (i.e., the simplification should not depend on whether multiplication was distributed into the rounding function argument).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Observed difference between versions: in sympy v1.5.1 both parse modes produced the same result:
- sympify(..., evaluate=False).simplify() -> 4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)
- sympify(..., evaluate=True).simplify()  -> 4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)

In sympy v1.6.2 the behavior is inconsistent:
- sympify(..., evaluate=False).simplify() -> 4*ceiling(x/4) - 3
- sympify(..., evaluate=True).simplify()  -> 4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)

(The inconsistency is due to expansion/distribution occurring into the rounding function argument on one parse/eval path but not the other.)

hints_text:
`4*ceiling(x/4) - 3` is simply wrong:
```python
>>> x = Symbol('x')
>>> (4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)).subs({x:0})
0
>>> (4*ceiling(x/4) - 3).subs({x:0})
-3
```
Boiling the problem further down we find that already a simpler expression is evaluated/transformed incorrectly:
```python
>>> sympy.sympify('ceiling(x-1/2)', evaluate=False)
ceiling(x) + (-1)*1*1/2
```
The `-1/2` is (under `evaluate=False`) constructed as `Mul(-1, Mul(1, Pow(2, -1)))`, for which the attribute `is_integer` is set incorrectly:
```python
>>> Mul(-1,Mul(1,Pow(2,-1,evaluate=False),evaluate=False),evaluate=False).is_integer
True
```
Since `ceiling` takes out all integer summands from its argument, it also takes out `(-1)*1*1/2`. Maybe somebody else can look into the problem, why `is_integer` is set wrongly for this expression.
The reason `is_integer` is incorrect for the expression is because it returns here:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/1b4529a95ef641c2fc15889091b281644069d20e/sympy/core/mul.py#L1274-L1277
That is due to
```julia
In [1]: e = Mul(-1,Mul(1,Pow(2,-1,evaluate=False),evaluate=False),evaluate=False)                                                              

In [2]: fraction(e)                                                                                                                            
Out[2]: (-1/2, 1)
```
It seems that the `1/2` is carried into the numerator by fraction giving a denominator of one.

You can see the fraction function here:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/1b4529a95ef641c2fc15889091b281644069d20e/sympy/simplify/radsimp.py#L1071-L1098

The check `term.is_Rational` will not match an unevaluated `Mul(1, Rational(1, 2), evaluate=False)` so that gets carried into the numerator.

Perhaps the root of the problem is the fact that we have unflattened args and `Mul.make_args` hasn't extracted them:
```julia
In [3]: Mul.make_args(e)                                                                                                                       
Out[3]: 
⎛      1⎞
⎜-1, 1⋅─⎟
⎝      2⎠
```
The `make_args` function does not recurse into the args:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/1b4529a95ef641c2fc15889091b281644069d20e/sympy/core/operations.py#L425-L428

I'm not sure if `make_args` should recurse. An easier fix would be to recurse into any nested Muls in `fraction`.
What about not setting `is_integer` if `evaluate=False` is set on an expression or one of its sub-expressions? Actually I think one cannot expect `is_integer` to be set correctly without evaluating.
That sounds like a good solution. As a safeguard, another one is to not remove the integer summands from `ceiling` if `evaluate=False`; it could be considered as an evaluation of ceiling w.r.t. its arguments.
There is no way to tell if `evaluate=False` was used in general when creating the `Mul`. It's also not possible in general to know if evaluating the Muls would lead to a different result without evaluating them. We should *not* evaluate the Muls as part of an assumptions query. If they are unevaluated then the user did that deliberately and it is not up to `_eval_is_integer` to evaluate them.

This was discussed when changing this to `fraction(..., exact=True)`: https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/19182#issuecomment-619398889

I think that using `fraction` at all is probably too much but we should certainly not replace that with something that evaluates the object.
Hm, does one really need to know whether `evaluate=False` was used? It looks like all we need is the expression tree to decide if `is_integer` is set to `True`. What about setting `is_integer=True` in a conservative way, i.e. only for these expression nodes:

- atoms: type `Integer`, constants `Zero` and `One` and symbols with appropriate assumptions
- `Add` and `Mul` if all args have `is_integer==True`
- `Pow` if base and exponent have `is_integer==True` and exponent is non-negative

I probably missed some cases, but you get the general idea. Would that work? The current implementation would only change in a few places, I guess - to a simpler form. Then also for `ceiling` we do not need to check for `evaluate=False`; its implementation could remain unchanged.
What you describe is more or less the way that it already works. You can find more detail in the (unmerged) #20090  

The code implementing this is in the `Mul._eval_is_integer` function I linked to above.
@oscarbenjamin @coproc Sorry for bugging, but are there any plans about this? We cannot use sympy with Python 3.8 anymore in our package because of this...
I explained a possible solution above:

With that we get:

```python
>>> e = Mul(-1,Mul(1,Pow(2,-1,evaluate=False),evaluate=False),evaluate=False)
>>> fraction(e)

>>> Mul(-1,Mul(1,Pow(2,-1,evaluate=False),evaluate=False),evaluate=False).is_integer
False
>>> sympy.sympify('ceiling(x-1/2)', evaluate=False)
ceiling(x + (-1)*1*1/2)
>>> sympy.sympify('4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)', evaluate=False).simplify()
4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)
>>> sympy.sympify('4*ceiling(x/4 - 3/4)', evaluate=True).simplify()
```

Thank you both so much!
As a general rule of thumb, pretty much any function that takes a SymPy expression as input and manipulates it in some way (simplify, solve, integrate, etc.) is liable to give wrong answers if the expression was created with evaluate=False. There is code all over the place that assumes, either explicitly or implicitly, that expressions satisfy various conditions that are true after automatic evaluation happens. For example, if I am reading the PR correctly, there is some code that very reasonably assumes that Mul.make_args(expr) gives the terms of a multiplication. This is not true for evaluate=False because that disables flattening of arguments. 

If you are working with expressions created with evaluate=False, you should always evaluate them first before trying to pass them to functions like simplify(). The result of simplify would be evaluated anyway, so there's no reason to not do this.

This isn't to say I'm necessarily opposed to fixing this issue specifically, but in general I think fixes like this are untenable. There are a handful of things that should definitely work correctly with unevaluated expressions, like the printers, and some very basic expression manipulation functions. I'm less convinced it's a good idea to try to enforce this for something like the assumptions or high level simplification functions. 

This shows we really need to rethink how we represent unevaluated expressions in SymPy. The fact that you can create an expression that looks just fine, but is actually subtly "invalid" for some code is indicative that something is broken in the design. It would be better if unevaluated expressions were more explicitly separate from evaluated ones. Or if expressions just didn't evaluate as much. I'm not sure what the best solution is, just that the current situation isn't ideal.
I think that the real issue is the fact that `fraction` is not a suitable function to use within the core assumptions system. I'm sure I objected to it being introduced somewhere.

The core assumptions should be able to avoid giving True or False erroneously because of unevaluated expressions.
In fact here's a worse form of the bug:
```julia
In [1]: x = Symbol('x', rational=True)                                                                                                                                            

In [2]: fraction(x)                                                                                                                                                               
Out[2]: (x, 1)

In [3]: y = Symbol('y', rational=True)                                                                                                                                            

In [4]: (x*y).is_integer                                                                                                                                                          
Out[4]: True
```
Here's a better fix:
```diff
diff --git a/sympy/core/mul.py b/sympy/core/mul.py
index 46f310b122..01db7d951b 100644
--- a/sympy/core/mul.py
+++ b/sympy/core/mul.py
@@ -1271,18 +1271,34 @@ def _eval_is_integer(self):
             return False
 
         # use exact=True to avoid recomputing num or den
-        n, d = fraction(self, exact=True)
-        if is_rational:
-            if d is S.One:
-                return True
-        if d.is_even:
-            if d.is_prime:  # literal or symbolic 2
-                return n.is_even
-            if n.is_odd:
-                return False  # true even if d = 0
-        if n == d:
-            return fuzzy_and([not bool(self.atoms(Float)),
-            fuzzy_not(d.is_zero)])
+        numerators = []
+        denominators = []
+        for a in self.args:
+            if a.is_integer:
+                numerators.append(a)
+            elif a.is_Rational:
+                n, d = a.as_numer_denom()
+                numerators.append(n)
+                denominators.append(d)
+            elif a.is_Pow:
+                b, e = a.as_base_exp()
+                if e is S.NegativeOne and b.is_integer:
+                    denominators.append(b)
+                else:
+                    return
+            else:
+                return
+
+        if not denominators:
+            return True
+
+        odd = lambda ints: all(i.is_odd for i in ints)
+        even = lambda ints: any(i.is_even for i in ints)
+
+        if odd(numerators) and even(denominators):
+            return False
+        elif even(numerators) and denominators == [2]:
+            return True
 
     def _eval_is_polar(self):
         has_polar = any(arg.is_polar for arg in self.args)

+++ b/sympy/core/tests/test_arit.py
@@ -391,10 +391,10 @@ def test_Mul_is_integer():
     assert (e/o).is_integer is None
     assert (o/e).is_integer is False
     assert (o/i2).is_integer is False
-    assert Mul(o, 1/o, evaluate=False).is_integer is True
+    #assert Mul(o, 1/o, evaluate=False).is_integer is True
```
