problem_statement.Title: parse_mathematica fails to parse Unicode identifier characters (e.g. 'λ')
problem_statement.Description: parse_mathematica in sympy.parsing.mathematica fails to accept Unicode identifier characters (such as 'λ'), raising a SyntaxError during parsing/tokenization. The failure manifests when tokenization does not recognize non-ASCII letters as valid identifier starts.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/parsing/mathematica.py:MathematicaParser._literal — Wrong assumption: identifiers start with ASCII letters only. Encoded value: "[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9]*". Correct relationship/value (per investigation): identifier start should be a Unicode letter and subsequent chars letters/digits (exclude underscore as first char). Exact replacement proposed: "[^\\W\\d_][^\\W_]*".

2) sympy/parsing/mathematica.py:MathematicaParser.RULES['whitespace'] and other RULES entries (e.g. RULES['add*_1'], RULES['add*_2'], RULES['Pi']) — Wrong assumption: character-class checks use ASCII-only classes [a-zA-Z] or [^a-zA-Z] (or [a-zA-Z\d]). These are used for omitted '*' insertion, boundary detection (e.g. Pi), and number/identifier adjacency. Exact Unicode-aware fragments proposed: left lookbehind fragment: "(?:(?<=[^\\W\\d_])|(?<=\\d\.))"; right lookahead fragment: "(?:(?=[^\\W\\d_])|(?=\\.\\d))"; or using non-word boundaries like "(?:(?:\\A|(?<=[^\\w])))" and end checks "(?=(?:[^\\w]|$))". Patterns that currently contain "(?=[(a-zA-Z])" or "(?<=[a-zA-Z])" should be updated to use Unicode-aware forms (e.g. "(?=[(\\w])" or "(?=[(^\\W\\d_)])" / "(?<=[^\\W\\d_])").

3) sympy/parsing/mathematica.py:MathematicaParser.FM_PATTERN — Wrong assumption: function-head/name detection and boundaries use ASCII-only classes; original example uses lookbehind "(?<=[^a-zA-Z])" and head pattern "[A-Z][a-zA-Z\d]*". This prevents recognition when surrounding/contained identifiers use Unicode letters. Exact proposed compilation example (Unicode-aware):
re.compile(r'''(?:(?:\\A|(?<=[^\\w]))) [A-Z][\\w]* (?=\[)''', re.VERBOSE | re.UNICODE)
Note: the initial "[A-Z]" was suggested to be kept to preserve built-in-function matching, but if Unicode uppercase function-heads are desired the initial class should be broadened to "[^\\W\\d_]".

4) sympy/parsing/mathematica.py:MathematicaParser.ARGS_PATTERN_TEMPLATE — Wrong assumption: argument boundary tests use [^a-zA-Z] for non-letter boundaries. Exact Unicode-aware fragments proposed: use "(?:(?:\\A|(?<=[^\\w])))" for left-boundary context and "(?=(?:[^\\w]|$))" for right-boundary context; compile templates with re.VERBOSE | re.UNICODE.

5) Tokenizer + downstream logic dependency — Locations: _get_tokenizer, _is_op, _from_mathematica_to_tokens — Problem: these parts rely on _literal (and the RULES that use ASCII classes) for token recognition and operator/literal classification. Because _literal is ASCII-only, tokens consisting of Unicode letters (e.g. 'λ') are never matched and downstream logic raises a SyntaxError ("unable to create a single AST for the expression").

6) Investigation limitations affecting confidence — The ASCII-only assumptions above were identified only within sympy/parsing/mathematica.py; the agent did not search the entire repository for similar ASCII-only regexes and did not run the test-suite or execute concrete tokenization traces to show runtime token lists. These gaps mean other files could encode the same incorrect assumption and the proposed replacements have not been validated by tests or CI runs.
StepsToReproduce: 1) In a Python environment with SymPy v1.11+, run: from sympy.parsing.mathematica import parse_mathematica
3) Observe that a SyntaxError is raised (traceback ends with: SyntaxError: unable to create a single AST for the expression)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: parse_mathematica('λ') returns the corresponding SymPy symbol named 'λ' (i.e., a Symbol with name 'λ') without raising a SyntaxError; more generally, Unicode identifier characters (Greek letters and other Unicode letters) are accepted as valid identifiers.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling parse_mathematica('λ') raises a SyntaxError ("unable to create a single AST for the expression") because the tokenizer/regexes in sympy/parsing/mathematica.py fail to recognize 'λ' as a valid identifier start (ASCII-only [A-Za-z] classes are used).

problem_statement.Title: TensorProduct.expand(tensorproduct=True) fails to fully distribute when factors include commutative (scalar) multipliers
problem_statement.Description: Calling .expand(tensorproduct=True) on a TensorProduct whose factor(s) include commutative (scalar) multiplicative factors (e.g. 2*U - V) can stop expanding prematurely. The incomplete expansion manifests in sympy.physics.quantum.TensorProduct expansion routines and affects downstream functions that rely on full tensorproduct expansion.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Too-narrow type check in TensorProduct._eval_expand_tensorproduct (sympy/physics/quantum/tensorproduct.py::TensorProduct._eval_expand_tensorproduct): the code constructs tp = TensorProduct(...) and only recurses when isinstance(tp, TensorProduct). This assumes the constructor will always return a TensorProduct instance, which is false.

2) Constructor behavior that pulls out commutative factors (sympy/physics/quantum/tensorproduct.py::TensorProduct.__new__): __new__ returns one of: c_part (a pure commutative scalar), c_part * new_args[0] (when only one non-commutative new arg), or c_part * Expr.__new__(cls, *new_args). Therefore tp can be a Mul of commutative factors and a TensorProduct (e.g. Mul(2, TensorProduct(...))) or a plain scalar; client code must handle these forms.

3) Missing use of args_cnc to detect scalar wrapping (sympy/core/expr.py::Expr.args_cnc): the correct way to split commutative and non-commutative parts is c_part, nc_part = tp.args_cnc(). Relying on isinstance(tp, TensorProduct) misses the case tp == Mul(c_part, TensorProduct(...)).

4) Correct recursion/distribution pattern that _eval_expand_tensorproduct must implement: after c_part, nc_part = tp.args_cnc(), if len(nc_part) == 1 and isinstance(nc_part[0], TensorProduct) then the code must recurse on the inner TensorProduct via expanded_nc = nc_part[0]._eval_expand_tensorproduct(); if expanded_nc is an Add, distribute Mul(*c_part) across expanded_nc.args (append Mul(*c_part)*term for each term), otherwise append Mul(*c_part) * expanded_nc. The current code neither performs this splitting nor this distribution, causing the expansion to stop early.

5) Edge forms not currently covered by the implementation: the code does not handle cases where nc_part has length != 1 or where the non-commutative part is itself wrapped in Pow/Mul of non-commutative factors (e.g. Pow(TensorProduct(...), n) or more complex Mul of non-commutative factors). These cases were identified by inspection but not exhaustively tested during the investigation.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import *; from sympy.physics.quantum import *; U = Operator('U'); V = Operator('V'); P = TensorProduct(2*U - V, U + V); print(P); print(P.expand(tensorproduct=True))"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The tensor product expansion fully distributes scalars and sums across tensor factors. For the example above the expanded result should represent the fully distributed sum of tensor products (i.e. the distribution of (2*U - V) over (U + V) producing all cross-terms with scalars applied to each resulting TensorProduct term).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Expansion stops prematurely when an intermediate TensorProduct instance produced by the constructor is wrapped by commutative scalar factors (Mul). For the example, calling .expand(tensorproduct=True) yields: 2*Ux(U + V) - Vx(U + V) (the second tensor factor is not fully distributed), i.e. the expansion does not recurse into scalar-wrapped TensorProduct parts.

problem_statement.Title: UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension rejects dimensionally-equivalent addends due to structural equality check
problem_statement.Description: When collecting factors and dimensions for Add expressions, UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension raises a ValueError if the Dimension objects are not structurally equal even when they are canonically dimensionally equivalent (e.g., Dimension(velocity) vs Dimension(acceleration*time)).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) UnitSystem uses structural equality to test addend compatibility (sympy/physics/units/unitsystem.py: UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension, Add branch). The code compares dimensions with `if dim != addend_dim:` and raises on inequality. This rejects cases where dimensions are canonically equivalent but structurally different (e.g., acceleration*time vs velocity). The correct compatibility test is the DimensionSystem canonical equivalence: `self.get_dimension_system().equivalent_dims(dim, addend_dim)`.

2) SI initialization calls DimensionSystem.equivalent_dims as if it were a classmethod (sympy/physics/units/systems/si.py). The call `DimensionSystem.equivalent_dims(_dimension, Dimension(dimex))` is incorrect because equivalent_dims is an instance method dependent on a DimensionSystem instance; the canonical check must be performed via the appropriate DimensionSystem instance (e.g., `SI.get_dimension_system().equivalent_dims(...)`).

3) Dimension.__add__ encodes a residual structural-equality assumption (sympy/physics/units/dimensions.py: Dimension.__add__). It allows addition only when `self == other` (structural equality), which can block direct Dimension + Dimension additions even when the DimensionSystem would consider them equivalent (e.g., Dimension(velocity) vs Dimension(acceleration*time)).

4) Quantity._collect_factor_and_dimension delegates to UnitSystem behavior (sympy/physics/units/quantities.py: Quantity._collect_factor_and_dimension), so the expression-level structural check in UnitSystem propagates to Quantity-level operations. Evidence for (1) is the reproduced traceback: `ValueError: Dimension of "v1" is Dimension(velocity), but it should be Dimension(acceleration*time)`.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following Python snippet with sympy installed:

from sympy.physics import units
from sympy.physics.units.systems.si import SI

v1 = units.Quantity('v1')
SI.set_quantity_dimension(v1, units.velocity)
SI.set_quantity_scale_factor(v1, 2 * units.meter / units.second)

a1 = units.Quantity('a1')
SI.set_quantity_dimension(a1, units.acceleration)
SI.set_quantity_scale_factor(a1, -9.8 * units.meter / units.second**2)

t1 = units.Quantity('t1')
SI.set_quantity_dimension(t1, units.time)
SI.set_quantity_scale_factor(t1, 5 * units.second)

expr1 = a1*t1 + v1
SI._collect_factor_and_dimension(expr1)
Observe the ValueError raised about mismatched dimensions (velocity vs acceleration*time).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Adding or collecting terms whose physical dimensions are dimensionally equivalent (even if their Dimension objects differ structurally) succeeds and returns the combined numeric factor and a canonical Dimension; no ValueError is raised for canonical equivalence (e.g., acceleration * time + velocity is allowed).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError is raised when collecting an Add expression whose summands have structurally different Dimension objects that are nonetheless dimensionally equivalent. Example observed message: `ValueError: Dimension of "v1" is Dimension(velocity), but it should be Dimension(acceleration*time)`.

problem_statement.Title: Prefix × Unit collapses to 1 when scale factors multiply to 1 (e.g. milli * W == 1)
problem_statement.Description: Multiplying a Prefix by a Quantity can produce the bare number 1 (dropping the unit) when the product of their numeric scale_factors equals 1. Example: milli * W evaluates to 1 instead of a prefixed watt (mW).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect early-return in Prefix.__mul__ (sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py:Prefix.__mul__): the method computes fact = self.scale_factor * other.scale_factor and immediately returns the bare integer 1 when fact == 1, regardless of whether `other` is a Quantity (unit). This discards unit information. Concrete values: milli.scale_factor == 10**-3 and W.scale_factor == 1000, so fact == 1 and the code returns 1.

2) Same incorrect assumption in Prefix.__truediv__ (sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py:Prefix.__truediv__): it performs an analogous check (fact == 1) and can collapse prefix / quantity to 1 in division contexts.

3) Derived-unit scale_factors propagate non-1 factors into units (sympy/physics/units/definitions/unit_definitions.py and sympy/physics/units/systems/length_weight_time.py): kilogram is registered as 1000·gram (kg.scale_factor == 1000), so derived units built from kg (newton, joule, watt) inherit that 1000 scale_factor. This makes W.scale_factor == 1000 and enables numeric cancellation with milli (10**-3).

4) Existing registration of canonical prefixed Quantities is available but ignored by Prefix methods (sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py:prefix_unit and UnitSystem._quantity_scale_factors_global): prefix_unit creates and registers prefixed Quantity objects and UnitSystem._quantity_scale_factors_global maps quantities → (mul_factor, reference_quantity). The Prefix.__mul__/__truediv__ logic does not consult this mapping before returning 1, so it fails to return the canonical prefixed Quantity (e.g., milliwatt) when appropriate.

5) Operator-dispatch behavior means Prefix methods handle both operand orders (Prefix._op_priority is higher than Quantity), so the fault in Prefix.__mul__/__truediv__ explains both milli * W and W * milli variations.

6) Known gap in investigation: some units may not have a registered canonical prefixed Quantity; in such cases the correct behavior would be to return a prefixed Quantity when available or numeric_factor * Quantity as the semantic result, not the bare number 1. This fallback behavior and interactions with custom UnitSystem instances were not exhaustively tested.
StepsToReproduce: Evaluate milli * W and observe the result (e.g., returns 1). Optionally evaluate milli*W == 1 to confirm equality and W * milli to see the asymmetry.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Multiplying a prefix by a unit should produce the corresponding prefixed unit (user-visible prefixed Quantity) or an expression representing that prefixed Quantity (for example, milli * W should evaluate to milliwatt or an equivalent Quantity representing mW). The unit must not be replaced by the bare number 1 when scale factors numerically cancel.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When the numeric product of prefix.scale_factor and unit.scale_factor equals 1, the operation returns the bare number 1 (unit information lost). Example: milli * W returns 1 because milli.scale_factor == 10**-3 and W.scale_factor == 1000. In other operand orders the result can appear as a unit multiplied by a Prefix object (e.g., watt*Prefix(milli, m, -3, 10)), showing inconsistent handling of operand order.
